None of this is needless bureaucracy. Every single rule points toward the same goal: turning invisible risks into visible data, and turning potential errors into inevitable discoveries.

I plan to hold a departmental meeting next Monday to announce the new standards. Everyone will be given a 3-day transition period, with formal implementation starting July 15.

# May 8, 2022, past 10 PM

Sitting in the study, it’s already very quiet outside. Meifang fell asleep a long while ago; I can hear her light snoring drifting in from the bedroom. Her blood pressure was a bit high again today. I saw the reading she took this afternoon, but I didn't tell her.

What happened at the chess room today keeps spinning in my head.

Midway through the game, I suddenly realized I was staring blankly at the board. My opponent asked what I was thinking about, and I said, "I'm getting older; my reactions aren't as sharp anymore." As soon as the words left my mouth, they felt foreign to me. I wouldn't have said that before. I would have said, "This is an interesting move," or I wouldn't have explained at all and just played the piece.

But today, I was just slow. The calculations in my head felt sluggish. I could feel it.

Player A tried to comfort me, saying my game has always been stable. I heard myself reply: "It’s stable because I’m afraid to change."

I regretted saying it immediately. Not because it wasn't true, but because it was *too* true. I’ve never been that blunt before. Stable... yes, I’ve always chased stability. Forty years, the same factory, the same way of working, the same rhythm of life. No job-hopping, no risks, every step planned out.

That’s not stability. That’s stagnation.

I left the chess room halfway through. That has never happened before. My friends were a bit surprised; I told them I was tired and needed to go home. As I turned to leave, I looked back at that place—the familiar boards, the familiar faces, everything so familiar. My eyes stung a little.

Why is this happening?

The tools in the workshop are still there. All these years, I’ve been wiping them down and organizing them, like I’m looking after something precious. But Hao is in real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou. They’ve never asked what those tools are for. Once, I tried to explain the use of a specific drill bit to Hao, but before I could finish, he said, "Dad, I’ve got a client I need to call back."

I’m recording the purpose of every tool in a notebook. Writing them down, one by one. But halfway through, I asked myself—what’s the point? Who is ever going to look at this notebook?

The physical exam report is still sitting in the drawer. The doctor said those figures "require attention." Every two days, I take it out and look at it, staring at the numbers, trying to find some kind of pattern. As if by doing that, I could control something. But the truth is, I can’t control anything. Blood pressure rises, blood sugar fluctuates, reactions slow down. These things are happening, and all I can do is record them.

Meifang’s blood pressure is rising too. I’m monitoring her diet and pushing her to get checkups. But what if one day I can’t move either? In our thirty-five years together, we’ve said too little. Now, even if I want to make up for it, I can't.

How much time is left?

I don't know. But I can feel time accelerating. It’s as if I used to have plenty of time to do something, and now I suddenly realize it’s gone. Retirement in two years, and then what? Empty days, no work, no one wanting anything from me, no one inheriting anything I know.

I sit here, pen in hand, but I don’t know what to write.

Maybe it’s best to just keep working tomorrow. At least when I’m working, I still know what I’m doing.

April 28, 2022, 11:15 PM

I’m sitting in the study. The lights are off. It’s pitch black outside the window.

I’ve been sitting here in the dark for a long time. My hand is pressed against my chest; I can feel my heartbeat. The rhythm is irregular. My breathing is a bit shallow, but I haven't moved. I just sit here, feeling it all.

The physical exam report is still on the desk. Blood pressure 139/88, fasting blood sugar 6.8. There’s a waveform on the ECG that the doctor said needs observation. He used the phrase "requires attention." I heard him loud and clear.

I’m thinking: this is how it begins.

I press my fingers against my pulse and count—76 beats per minute. Within the normal range. But I know it was racing just a moment ago. Why did it speed up? No exercise, nothing special happened. I was just sitting here when I suddenly felt... something I can't put into words.

Hao called today. He said the mortgage pressure is heavy and he might have to pay back a little less this month. I didn't say much; I just noted it down in my ledger. The mortgage, his sales commissions, the fluctuations of Shenzhen’s housing prices. I track all these data points. But what’s the use of tracking? Can I change anything?

The tools in the workshop are still on the shelves. Lately, I’ve been numbering them, writing down their uses and the years they were purchased. Some were bought in 1995, others in 2008. I wonder, if I were suddenly gone, who would know how to use these things? Not Hao. Not Yu. And certainly not Meifang.

They would just become scrap metal. Or be tossed out as junk.

I’ve been sitting in the dark for a long time, without turning on the light.

Tomorrow, I need to schedule a more detailed check-up. Cardiology. I need to know exactly what that abnormal waveform means. I need to establish a long-term monitoring plan. Every month’s data must be recorded so I can see the trends.

I also need to keep supervising Meifang’s blood pressure. She’s started eating salty foods again; I have to remind her once more.

My breathing has settled down a bit now.

But that sense of unease remains.

It’s as if something is slowly coming loose, and I can’t reach out to catch it.

# Diary

**Thursday, June 29, 2023 | Sunny**

This morning, I didn’t do what I usually do—I didn’t open my phone first thing to check those medical exam results. I don’t know why, but that sense of urgency just wasn't there today. Maybe I was too tired yesterday, or maybe… I can’t quite put my finger on it.

After getting out of bed, I went straight into the workshop. The tools were still there, exactly as they’ve always been. I picked up the pliers and started wiping them down. My movements were slow, almost like I was performing some kind of ritual.

Meifang got up and asked what I was doing. I asked her if she wanted to join me. The moment the words left my mouth, I froze a little—in thirty-five years, when have I ever proactively invited her to do anything? She looked a bit surprised too, but she walked over anyway. We just sat there side by side. I handed her a cloth, and she started wiping down another tool.

We didn’t say much. But the feeling… it was different.

Later, I thought back to that sharing session. That young engineer cried, you know? He said that what I talked about changed his understanding of precision machining. I didn’t pay much attention to it at the time; or rather, I was used to that kind of feedback. But thinking about it now, I realize that maybe I’ve been doing something very foolish—I locked everything away in my work. Locked it in that office, in those process improvement plans.

Since I stopped working, I’ve started to doubt whether these things still have any value. It’s a terrifying thought. But maybe… maybe the problem isn’t that these things have lost their value, but that I never saw where their true value actually lay.

I want to donate these tools to the school. I’d thought of the idea before, but I never followed through. Now, I really want to do it. Not to prove anything, and not to fill some void. Just… I want those young people to be able to use them. I want them to know that someone cares about these details, about this craftsmanship.

Meifang asked me what I was thinking about. I said nothing, just wiping the tools. She laughed and said I’ve always been like this—saying it’s nothing while my head is full of thoughts.

Maybe she’s right.

It’s still just after 7:00 AM, and it’s bright outside. I still have a lot of tools to wipe.

# MEMO

**Monday, May 15, 2023, 09:00**

---

Know when it’s time to let go.

I said those words yesterday, and I’m still turning them over in my mind. My superior came by again to try and persuade me—talking about re-employment, consultancy roles, and so on. I didn't bother explaining further. This is the moment. Not a day too early, not a day too late.

The files are mostly organized. Flipping through them one by one, there are things I’ve looked at a dozen times but still can’t bring myself to throw away. That 2008 process improvement plan—I remember working on it for three months. It’s been standard operating procedure for ages now. No one remembers who wrote it. Nor do they need to.

Farewell party this afternoon. I’m not sure what to say. Forty years... say too much and it feels sentimental; say too little and it looks like I don't care. I’ll probably just keep it simple. Those young engineers, especially that intern—the look in their eyes... I didn't expect that kind of reaction.

Post-retirement schedule—
- The Monday-to-Friday framework is gone; I’ll need to adapt to that.
- Keep up with the chess; need to give my chess partners a heads-up.
- Donating the tools to the school; need to coordinate a time with Meifang.
- Keep the physical exam reports safe; those few indicators... the doctor said it’s nothing major, but I still need regular follow-ups.
- Chen Hao’s mortgage; need to call and check in on how he’s doing.
- Household accounts... can’t stop those; if anything, I need to be more meticulous now.

Meifang was cleaning my tools for me yesterday. I didn't say anything, but she sat there for a long time. She’s grown used to my detachment over the years; is she unsure of how to be around me now? Or... am I the one changing?

Without the professional identity, who am I?

That question stings a bit. But there’s no need to figure it all out right now. One step at a time.

To-do list:
- Complete final handover procedures.
- Pack personal belongings (not much; my desk was always clean).
- Have a brief meeting with the department.
- Head home early tonight.

Not anxious. Just... a bit empty.

---

*Written in the office, looking out at the old factory grounds. Forty years.*

Meifang, are you there?

I just stepped out of the meeting room. I handled things a bit differently today. There was an issue with the data from one of my subordinates, and instead of my usual "this is unacceptable," I sat down and went through it with him, asking how he thought we could improve it. He looked a bit surprised. I talked with him privately for a while afterward, too.

I’ve been thinking about what you said. "I knew you’d have this sooner or later"—when did you first notice?

I’ve been wondering lately if maybe I’ve had it wrong all along. Managing work, managing my health, managing everything... it feels like no matter how precise I am, it doesn't really protect me from anything. Have you noticed anything different about me lately?

No need to rush to reply. Just wanted to ask.

June 18, 2023. Sunny.

2:00 PM. My wife is downstairs; I am sitting in the study.

What just happened is still looping in my head. During lunch, Meifang brought out a box of pastries from the kitchen—the kind with high fat content. Without thinking, I blurted out, "The doctor said to watch your fat intake, your blood pressure..."

She glanced at me. She wasn't angry, but her voice was flat: "I know. But I want to eat it."

Then she turned and went back to the kitchen.

I sat at the dining table, feeling something heavy in my chest. It wasn't anger—far from it. It was... a sense of powerlessness.

I’ve been doing this constantly lately. Monitoring her diet, reminding her of check-up appointments, even thinking of the doctor’s advice to "stay active" the moment she picks up the remote. I thought I was taking care of her, but now... I’m not so sure.

The moment she refused me, I felt offended. It’s absurd. I know it’s absurd.

In the days before I retired, I was in control of everything at the office—processes, standards, every single detail. And now? Now I’m at home trying to exert control in the same way. Am I overcompensating for something?

I ask myself: Am I truly doing this for her own good, or am I just trying to maintain some sense of order? Because I lost my authority at work, am I trying to reclaim it at home?

Meifang has lived with me for thirty-five years. She knows her own body; she knows the doctor’s recommendations. She doesn't need me to manage her life like a production line.

This realization makes me feel very uncomfortable.

Perhaps I need to learn to let go. But I don’t know how. When you’re used to using order to cope with uncertainty, suddenly accepting chaos and a lack of control... it’s hard.

It’s quiet outside now. I can hear the sound of the faucet downstairs.

I think I should go down and say something to her. But I don’t know what to say.

September 17, 2022 Afternoon

My hands are still shaking.

It’s been three hours since I left the hospital. I’m sitting in my workshop, and my hands are still shaking. 110 mg/dL. Grade 2 hypertension. Carotid atherosclerosis. The doctor spoke these words with a calm tone, as if he were talking about the weather. But I heard him loud and clear. Every number felt like a nail being driven in.

I tell myself these are just data points. I’ve organized forty years of production reports; I know how to read data. But this time, the data is telling me that my body is failing.

My wife said, "I knew this would happen to you sooner or later." That hurt more than the diagnosis itself. When did she notice? I always thought I had everything under control—diet, routine, checkups. I was even secretly monitoring her blood pressure, terrified something might happen to her. And the result? I’m the one who collapsed first.

It’s ridiculous.

My wife urged me to adjust my pace of life. I told her, "I know. That’s exactly what I’m doing." But what am I doing right now? I’m in the workshop, repeatedly wiping down these tools. I’ve wiped the same screwdriver over a dozen times. My movements are forceful, as if I’m trying to scrub something off. I know it’s absurd. I’m using this to mask my anxiety. But I can’t stop.

The doctor said I’ll need long-term medication. "Long-term." That’s a heavy word.

I’m thinking about those precision tools—the ones I’ve collected for over twenty years. And those forty years of experience—the quality control standards, the process optimizations, the things I’ve never explicitly said but are etched into my brain. If I fall, what happens to all of it? I don’t have time to think about that, because the moment I do, I start to feel afraid.

I don’t want to tell Chen Hao and Chen Yu. Not yet. They’ll ask too many questions, and I don’t have the energy to answer them. Besides, I don’t know what I’d even say. "Your father is sick"? That sounds too dramatic. But if I don’t tell them, they’ll hear it from their mother sooner or later.

I need to make a plan. The doctor gave me a medication list; I’ve already noted it down. Regular follow-ups. Dietary adjustments. And... I don’t know what else. I listened to everything the doctor said, but right now, only one thought is stuck in my head: I’m running out of time.

I originally planned to retire in two years. Now that plan might have to change. Or maybe it can’t be changed at all. I don’t even know how much longer I can keep working.

It’s ironic. I’ve spent my whole life pursuing control. Precise management, clear plans, every step taken by the book. But my body won’t listen. The body has its own logic, and I can’t change it with willpower alone.

I put down the screwdriver. My hands are still shaking.

I have work tomorrow.

# August 2, 2022, 8:30 PM

Sitting in the study. My wife is watching TV in the living room, the volume turned down low. I intended to organize today’s exercise logs, but right now, I can’t do anything.

I lost at the chess club today. That rarely happens.

It wasn't because my skills have declined—though perhaps they have—it was because I simply couldn't focus. When my friend A asked what was wrong, I just... let it out. About the medical report. Blood pressure 147/92, blood sugar 6.8, and a slight abnormality on the ECG. I hadn't even figured out how to say it, and yet there it was.

That’s not like me.

A said it was fine, that many people are like this and I just need regular check-ups. His words sounded hollow. I know he meant well, but he doesn't understand at all. No one can. This isn't just about abnormal data.

I’ve been controlling everything. Recording my wife’s blood pressure every day, watching everything she eats, walking 8,000 steps myself, weighing my diet down to the gram, organizing all the data into spreadsheets. I thought that by doing this, I could... I could stop something. But now I realize it’s all futile. Data can’t change anything.

Maybe I should retire early. I startled myself when I heard those words come out of my mouth. But once the thought appeared, I couldn't shake it. If my body is truly starting to fail, how much time do I have left? How much time is left for the things I haven't done yet?

The tools in the workshop, those ideas, and... I haven't even had a real conversation with Hao and Yu. They are in Shenzhen and Guangzhou, while I stay here recording data as if that were a form of love.

When I lost the game, I looked at the black and white pieces on the board and suddenly felt exhausted. Not a physical exhaustion, but something else.

It’s 8:30 PM. I have work tomorrow. Next week is the department’s quality assessment. Everything continues as if nothing has changed. But everything has changed.

I don’t know what to do. Or rather, I know what to do—make a plan, adjust the regimen, control the variables—but this time, I realize there are just too many things beyond my control.

Maybe I should sleep. I have to record the blood pressure data tomorrow.

Every decision I made at work, every time I insisted on quality—it’s all been influencing our company, and it’s been influencing my daughter. Her telling my stories in the classroom means my experience is living on. It’s different from what I thought before.

# Health Management and Lifestyle Adjustment Plan v2.0
**Date:** September 5, 2022  
**Status:** In Progress

---

## I. Exercise Plan (3 times per week)

| Item | Time | Frequency | Remarks |
|------|------|-----------|---------|
| Brisk Walk / Jog | Mon, Wed, Fri 6:30-7:00 | 3x per week | 30 mins, around the residential area |
| Gentle Stretching | 10 mins post-exercise | Every session | To prevent muscle stiffness |
| Weekend Activities | Sat / Sun | Optional | Chess and housework do not count as exercise |

**Execution Principles:**
- Focus on consistency rather than intensity.
- In case of bad weather, do simple exercises at home; do not skip.
- Log on the calendar; calculate completion rate monthly.

---

## II. Medical Check-up Schedule

**Frequency Adjustment:** Changed to **once every 6 months** (instead of frequent check-ups).

- First Follow-up: December 2022 (Focus on Cardiology)
- Second Follow-up: June 2023
- Subsequent: Maintain a semi-annual rhythm.

**Check-up Items:**
- ECG, Blood Pressure, Blood Lipids (Mandatory).
- Other indicators based on doctor’s advice; do not add tests voluntarily.

**Principle:** Regular monitoring without over-testing. Fluctuations in data are normal; weekly re-testing is unnecessary.

---

## III. Dietary Management Principles

**Allowed Foods:**
- Lean meats (chicken breast, fish, lean pork)
- Fresh vegetables and fruits (unlimited)
- Whole grains, soy products
- Clear soups, plain congee
- Tea, plain water

**Foods to Control:**
- Fried foods (Avoid completely)
- High-salt/cured foods (Max 2x per week)
- Sweets and sugary drinks (Max 1x per week)
- Tobacco and Alcohol (Tobacco: Strictly prohibited; Alcohol: Max 1 small glass per week)

**Implementation:**
- Li Meifang is responsible for shopping and cooking; I am responsible for supervision, not dictation.
- Plan menus weekly to avoid last-minute decisions.
- Limit dining out to once per week.

**Attitude Adjustment:** Healthy eating is a lifestyle, not a punishment. Occasional exceptions are permitted.

---

## IV. Psychological Counseling Action Items

**Tasks:**
- [ ] Consult the hospital’s psychology department or find a professional psychologist this week.
- [ ] Schedule the initial consultation (Target: Mid-September).
- [ ] Topics for consultation: Anxiety regarding aging, meaning of work, retirement planning.

**Expected Frequency:** 1-2 times per month; start with a 6-month trial period.

**Note:** This is not a sign of weakness, but a rational form of self-management—just like a physical check-up.

---

## V. Data Recording and Review

**New Recording Frequency:**

| Item | Frequency | Method |
|------|-----------|--------|
| Exercise Completion | Daily | Check off on the calendar |
| Blood Pressure | Once a week (Sun morning) | Record in the notebook |
| Weight | Once a week (Sun morning) | Record in the notebook |
| Monthly Summary | 1st of every month | Review execution and adjust next month's plan |

**Principles:**
- Stop measuring blood pressure and weight multiple times a day.
- Stop maintaining complex Excel spreadsheets.
- Simplify records; focus on trends rather than fluctuations.

---

## VI. Work and Retirement Planning

**Current Decision:**
- Do not retire early; continue working until age 65.
- Reason: Work itself has value; it is not an escape from aging.
- Find new meaning in work (e.g., mentoring younger staff, optimizing processes).

**Adjustments:**
- Maintain high standards for subordinates, but improve feedback methods (more constructive, less critical).
- Continue to propose improvement plans annually, but no longer just to "prove myself."

---

## VII. Family Relationship Maintenance

**With Li Meifang:**
- Stop authoritarian dietary supervision; switch to mutual discussion.
- Have at least one genuine conversation per week (not just listening to her talk about trivialities).
- Accompany her to her check-ups regularly, rather than monitoring her in secret.

**With Children:**
- Maintain monthly contact (phone or video call).
- Show concern for their lives without over-worrying.
- Consider inviting them home to increase quality time.

**Tool Donation:**
- Contact my alma mater (Industrial Technical School) to formally donate my precision tools.
- Accept this reality as another way of passing on knowledge.

---

## VIII. Monthly Checklist

**To be performed on the 1st of each month:**
- [ ] Review last month’s exercise completion rate (Target: 90%+).
- [ ] Check blood pressure and weight trends.
- [ ] Evaluate dietary compliance.
- [ ] Progress of psychological counseling (if applicable).
- [ ] Quality of interaction with family.
- [ ] Points of improvement at work.

**Handling Shortfalls:** Do not self-blame; instead, analyze the cause and adjust the plan for the following month.

---

## Remarks

This plan represents a shift from **extreme control** to **rational management**.

Previously, I tried to escape the fear of aging through perfection. Now I understand that while aging is inevitable, I can choose how to face it.

This plan is not about living longer, but about living with greater clarity.

# Wednesday, October 5, 2022, 14:20

Something slightly strange happened in the office this afternoon.

Xiao Wang made that same old mistake again—a discrepancy in the inspection report data entry. While it wasn't a fundamental error, by my standards, this kind of low-level oversight shouldn't happen. Sitting across from him, looking at that report, I already had my "straight-talking" critique ready in my head. But as the words reached the tip of my tongue, I stopped.

I asked him, "Do you think there’s another way to interpret this?"

He froze for a second, then started explaining his thought process. I waited until he finished before pointing out the issue. In the end, what I said was, "Let’s look at how we can improve this process together," instead of "This is unacceptable."

The look on his face visibly relaxed. I could tell.

What was I thinking? This isn't like me. I’ve always believed that strict standards drive better results—our defect rate being 25% lower than the industry average is proof of that. But today... it felt like I was conducting some kind of experiment. Seeing if gentle guidance could yield the same outcome.

Or rather, what am I trying to prove?

Before leaving work, I put together a "Quality Management Methodology Optimization Plan," documenting my observations on this change in feedback style. I used data to explain why this approach might be more efficient—reducing employee defensiveness and improving information retention, thereby lowering the rate of recurring errors. Writing it down that way made it feel more rational.

But I know that’s not the whole reason.

Lately, I feel like time is accelerating. Those numbers from my physical exam report are still spinning in my head. I find myself wondering: if one day I can no longer work the way I do now, how will people remember me? As a harsh quality control manager? Or... something else?

The thought is a bit absurd. I need to stay focused on management optimization, not these nebulous ideas.

I’ll continue to observe Xiao Wang’s performance tomorrow. Let’s see if this "experiment" actually works.

# Work Memo

**Date**: October 28, 2022  
**Subject**: Reflections on a New Approach to Feedback  
**Recorded by**: Chen Guilin  

---

## Record of Events

This morning, I discussed the process plan for a new product with a junior engineer from our department. While there were some lapses in rigor in his proposal, I refrained from my usual habit of pointing out the flaws directly.

**What I did differently**:
1. First, I acknowledged specific areas where he did well—the data collection was thorough, and the conceptual framework was clear.
2. I explained why precision control is critical at this stage, particularly regarding its impact on subsequent costs and yield.
3. Instead of providing the answer, I asked him: "Let’s look into this together—is there anything else we need to consider here?"
4. He identified the problem himself and even added a perspective I hadn't considered.

**Result**: His motivation was visibly boosted. He took the initiative to look up relevant materials after the meeting and returned this afternoon to discuss the improved plan.

---

## Reflections

This approach is a significant departure from my previous style. I used to be blunt about pointing out issues for the sake of efficiency. Looking back, however, that kind of feedback might lead people to passively accept criticism rather than truly think for themselves.

This time was different. He was involved in the thought process, which gave him more confidence in the final conclusion. More importantly, he didn't just learn the answer to this specific problem; he learned **how to analyze problems**.

---

## Follow-up Plans

If this method proves effective, I want to systematize it:

- Implement a new feedback framework within the department (Specific Praise → Explanation of Principles → Open Discussion).
- Compile a "Technical Mentorship Guide" in preparation for the knowledge hand-off before my retirement.
- Potentially organize a technical sharing session at the end of the year using this interactive format instead of a one-way lecture.

The benefit of this is that knowledge isn't just memorized—it is understood and internalized. Perhaps this is what true "legacy" looks like.

---

## Remarks

This idea stems from some recent reflections. My health is a reminder that I won't be in this position forever. Before I leave, can I ensure these young people inherit not just my standards, but my way of thinking? That might be more valuable than any procedural document.

I will continue to observe the results next week.

September 14, 2022, 10:00 PM

I just gave Hao a call.

I originally intended to ask if he was feeling the pressure of his mortgage or if he was short on cash lately. But the moment the call connected, I suddenly changed my tune. I asked him how he’s been doing and if there’s anything he needs help with.

He paused for a second; I could hear it in his voice. He probably found it strange—I never ask things that way. Usually, I just get straight to the point: work, income, mortgage progress. This time... for some reason, I just wanted to try a different approach.

We spoke for about five minutes. He said work has been okay, just that the recent heat has been a bit draining. I asked him if he’s been eating well and taking regular breaks. He laughed and said, "Dad, why the sudden concern?"

I didn't answer.

Before hanging up, I said, "Remember to let me know if you need anything." After I said it, even I was a bit stunned. Those are words I rarely ever say.

I’m sitting in the study now. The doctor’s diagnosis is still in the drawer. Grade 2 hypertension. Pre-metabolic syndrome. "Long-term medication." These terms have been spinning in my head all day, like a calculator that won't stop.

I always thought that through routine, through control, and through every precise number, I could keep everything held together. But the doctor said that’s not enough. Meifang’s words have also been ringing in my ears—"I knew you’d end up with this sooner or later." It’s as if she saw right through it all along, while I was still lying to myself.

Why did I suddenly want to check on Hao?

Maybe it’s because... I suddenly realized that for some things, time might be running out. The things I’ve always wanted to say but didn't; the areas I’ve always supervised but never truly cared about. For forty years, I’ve wiped down tools one by one in the workshop, established protocols in the office, and managed the accounts at home. But it seems I’ve never truly asked them what they actually need.

I was just controlling.

Now that I can’t even control my own body, I suddenly want to... change something. But change what? I don’t even know.

The thought is unfamiliar, and unsettling.

I have to go to the hospital to pick up my prescription tomorrow.

I have to find a way to document this tacit knowledge and turn it into something transferable. This, too, is a company asset.

Next week, I need to follow up on the May medical check-up reports. Li Meifang’s blood pressure has been fluctuating again this month; I must keep pushing her to watch her diet.

11:00 PM. Work log complete.

Tuesday afternoon, October 18, 2022

I spent the entire afternoon sitting in my small workshop. I didn’t clean any tools or organize my inventory. I just flipped through my notebooks, page by page.

What was I recording back then? Screwdriver models, wrench specifications, the purchase year and maintenance cycle for every single tool. Everything was neat and tidy, precise to a fault. Looking at these records used to give me a sense of peace—as if I had mastered something.

But today, I’m wondering: what did these tools actually teach me?

It wasn't how to use them. That’s too simple. It was... how to approach a task. With seriousness. Without compromise. Knowing that every detail matters. For forty years, the products I made with these tools had a defect rate 25% lower than the industry average. That wasn't a coincidence. It was because I never allowed myself to slack off.

But—

I wrote that word on a new page, then crossed it out. Wrote it again. Crossed it out again.

But I treated people the same way. My subordinates, my family. I demanded perfection and left no room for error. I thought that was called being responsible. Now, I’m not so sure.

Li Meifang has been humming in the kitchen lately. I haven't heard her hum like that in a long time. She says it’s because I’ve finally "loosened up a bit." I can hear it in her voice—it wasn't a compliment. It was... relief? She feels relieved.

That hurts a little.

I turned to a blank page and drew a timeline with a pencil. From 1982 to now. Forty years. I marked the key years: technician, team leader, department manager. I did well at every stage. Defect rates, process optimizations, the proposals that were adopted. It’s a beautiful report card.

But so what?

Chen Hao is in Shenzhen, under a lot of pressure. I keep track of his mortgage and his performance, but we barely have anything to say to each other. When Chen Yu comes home occasionally, our conversations are like status reports—how’s work, is your marriage stable. I thought I was teaching them about perseverance and quality awareness, but I was never truly there for them.

This is my regret. Perhaps my greatest regret.

Next to the timeline, I wrote: "If I could do it over, what would I do?"

But I know there are no "ifs." I only have the now. And about two years left of work. Maybe in these two years, I should record some things. Not tool lists, not process documents. But... what I was thinking during these forty years. Why I did things the way I did. Young people might need that.

Or rather, I need to do this. To forgive myself.

Meifang is calling me for dinner. I hear her voice drifting up from downstairs—gentle, not rushing me. I close the notebook and stand up.

Maybe I should listen to her more. Really listen, instead of mentally calculating her blood pressure and diet.

Maybe that is what I really need to learn.

## Incident Log

During dinner, I noticed Li Meifang adding another spoonful of salt to the braised fish. When asked why, she said, "It needs it for the flavor." I didn't say much at the time, but I immediately thought of last week’s physical exam report—her blood pressure was 139/88, up 5 points from last month.

This is not a trivial matter.

## Data Correlation

- Last month’s BP: 134/83
- This month’s BP: 139/88
- Fluctuation: +5 mmHg (Systolic)
- Likely triggers: Excessive sodium intake + irregular sleep schedule

A spoonful of salt may seem insignificant, but the cumulative effect is clear. Intervention is necessary.

## Action Plan

**Phase 1 (This week):**
- Purchase low-sodium salt to replace the current kitchen salt.
- Develop daily menus to limit sodium intake to ≤ 2000mg.
- Record daily blood pressure readings (once in the morning, once in the evening).

**Phase 2 (Starting next week):**
- Prepare a list of low-sodium recipes (3 rotating sets each for breakfast, lunch, and dinner).
- Reduce the use of pickled foods and sauces.
- Ensure she takes her blood pressure medication on time.

**Monitoring Schedule:**
- Check the seasoning of dishes every day before dinner.
- Record blood pressure data every Monday and Thursday.
- Compare results during next month’s follow-up.

## Points of Concern

The issue with Li Meifang is that she doesn't see this as a problem. She says, "It's just a little salt," but she doesn't understand the cumulative effect. I need her to realize that this isn't me being nitpicky—it's what the data shows.

I’ve already told her that I will help her implement this plan. It’s not about her keeping track; it’s about me taking control. That is the only way to ensure it works.

## Next Steps

A follow-up blood pressure check is scheduled for May 25. If it’s still rising, we’ll need to consider increasing her exercise or adjusting her medication. But first, the diet must be brought under control.

Time is of the essence. We need to manage these details while we still can.

---

# August 15, 2022, 3:20 AM

Can’t sleep. I’ve been awake for two hours now.

My chest still feels a bit tight; I can feel my heartbeat when I press down on it. My rational mind tells me this is anxiety, not a heart condition. But my fingers still instinctively press down several times, trying to be sure. My breathing is a bit shallow. I tell myself to take deep breaths—one, two, three, exhale—but the more I try to control it, the more labored it feels. It’s ridiculous.

I turned off my phone. Meifang knocked on the door and asked if I wanted some warm water. I didn't respond. She didn't come in eventually; I heard her footsteps slowly fading away. She’s used to me being like this. After 35 years, she knows all too well that I don’t like to be disturbed, especially at times like this. But I could sense her lingering outside the door for a few seconds. I feel a twinge of guilt, but I don't have the energy to deal with that right now.

I checked the Excel sheet again this afternoon. Meifang’s blood pressure readings for this week: 150/95, 148/92, 152/98… I marked three of them in red pen. I wrote "follow-up appointment needed" next to them, and then "dietary control." But I know these numbers don’t change anything. I can record them, I can mark them, I can make plans, but I cannot change the fact that her body is aging.

Last night, I searched for information on hypertension complications again. Stroke, myocardial infarction, kidney failure… page after page. I told myself to stop, that searching like this is pointless. But my fingers kept clicking. It’s as if something is driving me to confirm the worst-case scenarios, as if knowing in advance would somehow make me prepared. It’s stupid. I know perfectly well it’s stupid.

My own data sheets are there, too. Daily exercise time, step count, heart rate, blood sugar levels… all neatly arranged. It’s as if as long as these numbers stay in the green zone, I can guarantee that nothing will go wrong with me. But I know I’m lying to myself. I can’t stop aging by keeping records. I can’t guarantee my heart won’t fail through a strict diet. This realization terrifies me more than any abnormal data point ever could.

The physical exam is in two weeks. I’m both looking forward to it and dreading it. I’m looking for a definitive answer, yet I’m afraid that answer will confirm all the premonitions I’ve had over the past few months.

The tools in the workshop haven't been wiped down yet. I was supposed to organize those precision instruments today, but I just couldn't find the motivation. I sat there, looking at them, thinking that I might not have much time left. Time seems to be accelerating. Last year, retirement still felt far off; now, it feels like two years have passed in the blink of an eye.

No good news from the kids either. Hao is still under a lot of pressure with his mortgage, and Yu hasn't said much lately. I want to give them a call, but I don't know what to say. Ask them how their health is? That would be too strange. Or just listen to them talk about work in silence? There doesn't seem to be any other way for us to communicate anymore.

It’s not dawn yet. The study is very quiet. It’s just me and these data points, these records—these things I try to use to fight against this sense of powerlessness.

I think I need to sleep. But I know I won’t be able to.

Meifang, we tried a different approach in our department meeting today.

That new young engineer—I used to correct him so bluntly that he seemed a bit intimidated by me. Today, I started by pointing out what he did right before explaining why certain changes were needed. Surprisingly, he started asking a lot of questions and eventually figured it out on his own.

It worked quite well. He even came to me this afternoon on his own initiative to ask about a technical process.

It occurred to me that maybe this way of teaching is more effective than just pointing out mistakes. In the past... I might have been too impatient.

All these years you’ve spent listening to me talk about work, my tone hasn't always been the best. I really just wanted to teach things properly, but my methods might have been wrong.

Seeing that young man’s expression today reminded me of something you once said—sometimes people need to be seen, not just corrected.

What do you want for dinner? I’ll be home early.

September 29, 2022 | Sunny | 7:30 AM

I hesitated for a long time when I picked up the pill.

Standing there by the kitchen sink, clutching that small white tablet, I saw that Li Meifang had already placed a glass of water next to me. She didn’t say anything; she just stood there, and I could feel her watching me. This feeling of being watched is unfamiliar. For all these years, I was the one watching her—making sure she took her blood pressure medication on time, watching her diet, checking her physical exam reports. Now, it’s reversed.

I know what swallowing this pill signifies. It’s not that I didn’t understand the doctor’s diagnosis before, but picking up the pill and actually taking it feel like two different things. One is data; the other is... reality.

I marked the medication time on the calendar. I used that 0.5mm black gel pen; the handwriting is small and neat. I also set three reminders on my phone—7:00 AM, 12:00 PM, and 7:00 PM. That way, I won't miss a dose. I can manage this. Through precise records, through making no mistakes, maybe...

But I’m lying to myself. I know that.

Only after I swallowed it did Li Meifang turn away to start the congee. She didn’t ask how I felt, nor did she say anything encouraging. She just went about her business quietly. For some reason, that made me feel worse. If she had said something, I would have known how to respond. But she said nothing, as if she has already accepted this, while I am still... still what?

I stared at the empty pill bottle for a long time. I’ve read the manual three times now. All the data, the dosage instructions, every possible side effect—I’ve committed them all to memory. This shouldn't be hard. I managed processes at the factory for twelve years; I’ve handled problems far more complex than this. But this is different.

This time, I can’t solve it by improving the process. I can’t reverse it through stricter management standards.

The office clock is about to strike 8:00. I should head out. But I sit here, looking at that marked calendar, thinking about how this single pill represents more than just blood pressure—it represents the fact that time is quietly slipping away.

How many more 7:00 AMs do I have left?

Forget it. Don’t think like that.

I stand up and organize the documents on my desk. Business as usual. Time for work.

September 10, 2022, 4:45 PM

Just got back from the hospital. My hands are still shaking.

I wrote down everything the doctor said. Blood sugar at 110 mg/dL, systolic 160, diastolic 100—Stage 2 hypertension. And then that phrase: pre-metabolic syndrome. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. I asked him if he was sure; he said he was. Then he wrote out a stack of prescriptions and said I’d need long-term medication.

Long-term medication.

I kept poking at those three words on the prescription slip for the longest time. The tip of my pen nearly tore through the paper. The doctor probably noticed, but he didn't say anything.

Forty years. I’ve worked at this plant for forty years. Up at 6:30 AM, in bed by 10:00 PM. Never missed a day in my work log. Portioned meals, regular exercise, timely check-ups. I thought that would be enough. I thought that through precise management—by keeping everything under my thumb—my body wouldn't betray me.

Now the doctor is telling me it wasn't enough.

I always felt I was different from the others. Everyone else is so careless, while I am precise down to the minute. I thought that was an advantage. And now? Now I realize there are far more things I can’t control than things I can. The things happening inside my body—I can’t see them, I can’t touch them, and I can’t change them.

My wife said, "I knew you'd end up with this sooner or later." Her words made me angry, but I didn't argue back. Because I couldn't. She probably saw it coming long ago, while I was still lying to myself.

I need to start the medication. Every day. Probably for a long time. Maybe for the rest of my life.

How much longer is left? I keep wondering. If my body is already like this, how much longer can I work? Can I hold on for two more years until retirement? Or should I leave early? But what does early retirement even mean? What do I have left besides work?

All those tools in the workshop. Collected over forty years. And those process improvement plans. If I collapse, who takes over? Hao and Yu aren't in this industry. They might not even know what any of it is worth.

My head is a mess right now. I should calm down and make a plan. But I find that I can't. Because I don't know what I'm supposed to be planning for.

My hands are still shaking.

I need to put these pills in a fixed spot. Record the dosage every day. I can do that. I must do that.

But as for everything else?

I don't know.

# Work Log · Knowledge Repository

**Date:** July 7, 2023, 9:00 PM
**Recorded by:** Chen Guilin

---

## Case Study: Handling the 2010 Quality Crisis

I recently dug up some old work logs and came across the records from the 2010 crisis. Tonight, I want to systematically organize this case. It’s not about nostalgia; I just feel that these things shouldn't be left to rot in a drawer.

**Background:**
In March 2010, a major client reported batch-level weld cracking after two months of product use. The issue involved an order of approximately 8,000 units, with 6,500 already delivered. I was the Quality Control Manager at the time.

**The Decision-Making Process:**

Step 1—Immediate Damage Control (March 15–16)
- Halted all subsequent production and shipments for that batch.
- Initiated an emergency recall of delivered products (eventually recovered 4,200 units).
- Notified the client and explained the issue transparently rather than hiding it.
- Cost: A decline in client trust and a three-month freeze on orders.

Step 2—Root Cause Analysis (March 17–April 10)
- Determined it wasn't a design flaw, but a temperature control deviation in the welding process.
- Discovered that while the parameters on the newly replaced welding machines were set correctly, the actual power output fluctuated by ±3% from the standard.
- This fluctuation was overlooked during routine testing (our testing precision at the time was only ±5%).

Step 3—Systemic Improvements (April–June)
- Upgraded testing equipment, improving precision to ±1%.
- Fully standardized the welding process and established daily temperature logs.
- Retrained all QC inspectors, emphasizing a re-inspection mechanism for "borderline" products.
- Established an "Anomaly Early Warning" system—any time testing data approached the upper or lower limits, the process had to be traced.

**Results:**
- Production resumed in July, and supply was restored in August.
- The client eventually accepted our solution and compensation package.
- That client went on to become one of our most stable major accounts, and we continue to work together to this day.

**Distilled Principles:**

1. **Transparency over Concealment** — The decision to tell the truth back then was difficult. However, if we had hidden it and the client found out on their own, trust would have been gone forever. Honesty gave us a chance to make amends.

2. **Root Cause vs. Surface Cause** — It’s easy to blame "improper operator handling," but the real issue was the equipment's inherent fluctuation. If we had only punished the employees, the problem would have recurred.

3. **Testing Precision Must Lead the Process** — Our testing standard was ±5%, but the actual process fluctuation was ±3%. This meant our testing was essentially operating in a "blind spot." Since then, I’ve insisted that testing precision must be an order of magnitude higher than the process requirements.

4. **Early Warning vs. After-the-Fact Remediation** — Discovering a problem only after a crisis hits carries the highest cost. The core of quality management is establishing early warning mechanisms to catch issues in their infancy.

---

## Advice for Young Managers

If someone asked me how to handle quality management, I would say these few things:

**Don't just look at the numbers.** Low defect rates and high pass rates look great on paper. But what’s behind those numbers? Is the process truly stable, or are you just getting lucky? Frequently ask yourself: "If a link in this chain fails, how much lead time would I have to detect it?"

**Trust your subordinates, but don't trust the process.** My team has strong execution, but I never relax standards just because "they’ve never had a problem before." Process stability must be guaranteed by the system, not by individual conscientiousness.

**A crisis is worth its cost only if you gain systemic improvements.** In 2010, we spent three months and a significant amount of capital. But that early warning system has been in use ever since—how many potential crises has it prevented? It was worth it.

**Client relationships are forged in a crisis.** Routine cooperation is easy; how you handle things when they go wrong is what reveals a company's true character.

---

## Current Thoughts

It feels a bit strange sitting at home writing this. I’ve lived for forty years and never systematically organized these thoughts. When I was working, I always felt too busy; now, I finally have the time.

I’m wondering if I should compile these cases and reflections into a formal document. Not for fame or anything, but I just feel this experience shouldn't go to waste. If it can provide some reference for younger managers, it will have been of some use.

I mentioned donating tools to the school before; now I’m thinking, maybe I could also do a small knowledge-sharing session? It doesn't have to be formal—just talking through these cases.

I'll finish organizing these notes first.

---

**Next Steps:**
- [ ] Organize the 2015 supply chain optimization case.
- [ ] Organize the 2018 automation transformation experience.
- [ ] Consider whether to format these into a shareable document.

August 24, 2022, Evening

I said some things at the chess and card room today that I’m still regretting a little.

I played three games with Chess Friend A. I won the first two, but halfway through the third, I suddenly asked him—has everything I’ve done over the last forty years been meaningless?

After I said it, there was a long silence. No one responded; they just kept staring at the board. I knew the question was too heavy, that it wasn't something to be asked over a game of chess. But at that moment, I truly wanted an answer.

Eventually, he said that instead of spending time fearing the future, it’s better to spend time cherishing the present.

I didn't say anything else and kept playing. But those words have been spinning in my head ever since.

Lately, I’ve been dwelling on useless things. Ever since the physical exam report came back, I’ve started feeling like nothing matters anymore. All those tools in the workshop that I spent over twenty years collecting and organizing—now that I think about it, neither Haohao nor Yuyu will want them. Maybe I should donate them. Maybe that’s the right thing to do.

But after they’re gone, what will I have left?

Recording those data points every day, monitoring Meifang’s diet, checking the accounts, reading industry reports… what am I doing? What am I trying to control? I can’t control aging, I can’t control my body, and I can’t control my children’s lives.

Chess Friend A was right. I’ve been constantly fearing the future, fearing death, fearing that everything is meaningless. So I use control to fight that fear. But doing so has only turned the present into a nightmare as well.

I haven't cherished anything.

Meifang said her blood pressure was high again today, and I started nagging her about her diet again. I saw the exhaustion in her eyes. I saw it, but I didn't stop. I kept controlling, kept demanding, kept pretending I was doing it for her own good.

Maybe I should just sit with her for a while. Just sit there, without talking, without planning, without controlling.

But I don't know how.

As for the tools in the workshop, I might call the technical school tomorrow. They asked me before if I’d be willing to donate them. I never agreed because I felt I couldn't let go. Now, I feel like maybe it’s time.

It’s not because I’ve had some great epiphany. I’m just tired.

Too tired to keep controlling everything.

# October 19, 2022, Evening

After dinner, Meifang told me I’ve changed.

I didn’t deny it right away. I just sat there and listened as she went on—saying I’ve been talking more lately, that I’m not so quick to blame people anymore, and that I’ve started asking her how her day was. She spoke calmly, not as if she were praising me, but more like she was stating an observed fact.

I replied, "Maybe it’s time for a change."

Even as I said it, I felt a bit strange. This isn't the kind of thing I usually say. Normally, I would explain, defend myself, or use logic to prove my way was right. But today, I didn't.

Something happened at the office this afternoon. Xiao Li made another mistake—a dimensional error that never should have happened. Usually, I would have pointed it out in front of everyone, being blunt so he’d learn his lesson. But I stopped. I actually stopped myself.

I asked him, "Which part of the process went wrong this time?"

He nervously explained the whole workflow. I listened until he finished, then pointed out the issue and asked how he could avoid it next time. When he nodded, there was no look of humiliation in his eyes.

Thinking back on it tonight, I realized something—when I stopped blaming him, he was actually more willing to listen to me. This discovery makes me feel a bit uneasy.

For years, I believed that being harsh was being responsible, and being blunt was being honest. I managed the department this way; I treated my subordinates this way. And the result? Defect rates went down, but people left. It never occurred to me that the two might be cause and effect.

The doctor said my blood pressure and heart rate need managing. I’ve been on medication for a week, and my body does feel a bit more settled. But what’s truly changing me isn't the medicine; it’s this sense of powerlessness—the realization that I can’t control my body’s aging, I can’t control the passage of time, and I can’t control whether others understand me.

Maybe that’s the real transformation. It’s not that I’ve become gentler, but that I’ve finally admitted my own limitations.

As Meifang cleared the dishes in the kitchen, I sat in the living room and suddenly thought of my father. Did the technicians of his generation go through moments like this? Moving from believing they could control everything to accepting that some things are simply beyond their grasp?

I never asked him. And now, I can’t.

I have a department meeting tomorrow. I want to change the way I give feedback. I don’t know how it will go.

But at the very least, I’ve decided to try.

# May 11, Sunny

The physical exam report came back.

Blood pressure: 138/88. Higher than the 133/85 back in 2020. I did the math—systolic pressure is up by 3.7%. I wrote the numbers down on paper and calculated it again. It is indeed 3.7%.

Meifang has been taking blood pressure medication lately, the kind I bought for her. She says she doesn't feel much, just gets dizzy sometimes. I didn't say anything, but I made a mental note—I must urge her to go for another checkup next month. Her blood pressure is higher than mine.

Sitting in the office, I organized the last five years of my physical exam data into a table. 2018, 2020, 2022. The numbers lined up in a row on the paper. Looking at them, a strange question crossed my mind—why am I documenting my own aging with such precision?

During the meeting this afternoon, I proposed a process improvement plan. The new quality inspector hadn't been thorough enough, so I pointed it out in front of everyone. He turned red. I knew he was uncomfortable, but standards are standards; you can't lower requirements just to avoid hurting feelings. That has always been my principle.

But sitting in my office later, it suddenly occurred to me—how much longer can I keep demanding this level of rigor from others?

I wiped down the tools in the workshop once more. That vernier caliper I bought in 1985 is still as accurate as ever. In my notebook, I wrote down its purpose, precision, and range of application. I’ve already documented over thirty items. Hao is busy selling real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou. Neither of them will ever use these things.

I asked myself—why keep recording?

But I kept recording anyway. I poked a small hole in the corner of the notebook with the tip of my pen. Not with force, just a gentle poke. I did it several times.

Li Meifang asked me what I wanted for dinner. I said "whatever." She said we’d have something light then. I nodded.

Two years until retirement. The plan hasn't changed. But I feel the plan becoming stricter—every item must be more detailed, more precise, more intolerant of error. It’s as if I’m using more control to fight against something. But it doesn't change anything.

Time is still slipping away.

3.7%.

That’s all.

Haozi, how have you been lately?

I’ve been thinking, and I wanted to share a few words with you. This recent health scare has given me a lot to reflect on. The doctor said I need to stay on medication and slow down. With your mom here by my side, I’ve realized that all these years, I’ve been constantly rushing—rushing to keep everything under control, to make everything perfect.

Regarding your mortgage in Shenzhen, I know I used to pester you with questions, almost like I was auditing your books. Looking back, that wasn't right. You’re an adult now, and you have your own plans. I thought I was "helping" you in my own way, but in reality, I was just putting pressure on you.

I’ve only recently come to understand that maybe life doesn't need to be perfectly controlled. In my forty years of work, I always pursued "zero defects," but life isn't an assembly line. There are things you just can't control, and there’s pressure you have to learn to let go of. Otherwise, you’ll end up like me—waiting until your sixties to regret what you’ve missed out on.

I’m not telling you this so you’ll follow in my footsteps. On the contrary, I hope you can live a more relaxed life than I did. A mortgage is just a mortgage, and a job is just a job; don't keep yourself wound so tight. Taking time for family and getting proper rest—those things matter, too.

Your mom says I’ve become quite talkative lately, ha. Maybe I really am changing.

If you’re free this weekend, give me a call. Let’s have a good chat.

— Dad

September 20, 2022, 10:15 PM

Li Meifang said something tonight that I’m still thinking about.

Over dinner, I brought up work—the new QC inspector made another mistake, and I called him out on it directly. She put down her chopsticks, looked at me, and said, "You know, I knew you’d end up with this sooner or later." I didn't realize what she meant at first. Then she added, "Maybe your problem isn't physical; it’s that you never leave any room for error—not for others, and not for yourself."

I didn't answer. We kept eating, just like usual. But those words have been circling in my head ever since.

The doctor’s words from this afternoon are still ringing in my ears. 110, Grade 2, atherosclerosis. The numbers are clear. I always thought I had everything under control—diet, routine, exercise—but clearly, it wasn't enough. I don’t even know when it stopped being enough.

While I was wiping down my tools in the workshop, I thought that maybe Meifang is right. My attitude toward that new employee seems to be the same as my attitude toward my own body right now—demanding perfection, allowing no flaws. But perfection... I suddenly realized that maybe it doesn't even exist.

I told Meifang that being diligent might not be enough; maybe I need more patience. I was a bit surprised at myself for saying it. It felt strange coming out of my mouth. She just nodded and didn't say anything, but I could tell she was smiling.

I’m lying in bed now; my wife is already asleep. I’m wondering, if I can’t even fully control my own body, then what was the point of everything I’ve taken pride in for the last forty years—the precision management, the process optimizations?

Maybe the point isn't perfection itself, but... I haven't figured it out yet.

I have to go to the hospital tomorrow to pick up my prescription. The doctor said I’ll need long-term medication. I don’t like the word "long-term." But it seems I don't have a choice right now.

# Monday, July 18, 2022, 14:30

It’s been two hours since I got the report.

I’m sitting in my office with the report spread out on the desk. I told everyone "it’s nothing serious," but my fingers keep tracing those marked spots—blood pressure 139/92, fasting blood glucose 6.8. The numbers themselves don’t lie. I know that better than anyone.

I heard everything the doctor said, but I chose to remember only the first half of his sentences. "It’s under decent control." The rest—the parts about "needing to be more cautious" and "regular follow-ups"—I just let slide. That’s not my style.

Meifang was cooking when I got home. She saw the look on my face and asked about it. I didn't give her a straight answer, just said "I'm fine." But I know she can tell. After 35 years, she knows me too well. She tried to come closer, wanting me to talk, but I turned away and went into the study.

Then I suddenly turned back around and said to her, "Starting today, you need to cooperate too. We can't let your blood pressure issues drag on anymore. I’m going to set up a plan for you."

She was startled by my tone. I saw it. But I didn't stop. I need to control something. I need to ensure that something is within my grasp.

She isn't the problem. I know that. But at this moment, I can't face the fact that *I* am the problem.

Data doesn't lie. I’ve lived my whole life by data and standards, and now the data is starting to betray me. Blood pressure, blood sugar, weight—every indicator is slowly crossing the line. I can see the trend. I can predict it. And that is exactly what’s so terrifying.

The two-year retirement plan. I spoke of it with such certainty. But now I’m starting to wonder: if my body continues like this, how many "two years" do I have left?

The tools in the workshop are still there. I wanted to go and wipe them down, as I usually do. But today, I didn't move. I sat in my office, staring at this report, trying to use logic to convince myself that this is all within manageable limits.

But I know that some things are forever beyond my control.

For the first time, I truly felt that.

I had another minor disagreement with Meifang today over the salt. She added an extra spoonful while simmering the soup; I caught the scent and went over immediately to check. I poured her a glass of water and told her to drink it first. Then I told her I’d be putting together a low-sodium meal plan for her this weekend. She didn’t object; she just gave me a smile—that look of someone who’s grown used to my ways.

While sitting in the workshop, I flipped through the tool log again. I’ve already recorded over thirty items this year, each marked with its purpose and maintenance method. Sometimes I write "Who will use this?" in the margins—a question for myself. Hao is selling real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou; neither of them will ever touch these things. I know that.

But I keep recording anyway. While I can still move.

The May physical is coming up. The cardiologist mentioned last time that I need regular follow-ups. I haven't told the family exactly what it's for, just that it's a routine check-up. Meifang asked once, and I said, "Nothing major, just some fluctuations in the data." Honestly, I don't really want to dwell on it myself.

Tonight, I glanced at a WeChat message from Hao. He said the mortgage pressure is a bit heavy this month and asked if I could support him a little more. I transferred five thousand yuan to him without saying much. He replied with a "Thanks, Dad." That was it. I tried to remember the last time he came home—was it three months ago? Or four? I couldn't recall.

I wonder, should I keep doing all this after I retire? Should I keep keeping the accounts? Should I keep polishing the tools? Should I keep managing everything in this house?

I have to get everything done that needs doing, while I still can.

I have to hurry.

Perhaps this is what I can do now—share these things and give them meaning.

Regardless, I must get the workshop in order this weekend. Everything must be in its proper place.

The whole process was smoother than usual. My subordinate's level of cooperation improved, and there wasn't that sense of defensiveness that usually follows a reprimand.

# Work Log

**Date**: Friday, October 28, 2022
**Weather**: Sunny
**Recorded by**: Chen Guilin

---

## Today's Work

Held a departmental feedback meeting at 10:00 AM. The approach today was different from usual, and it’s worth noting down.

Previously, I would point out problems directly and give corrective feedback immediately. Today, I changed my mindset. I started by praising several engineers for their recent performance—specifically Xiao Wang’s ideas on improving the welding process. Only then did I explain why certain parameters needed to be adjusted this way instead of that way. The key was that I invited them into the thought process rather than just handing them the answers.

I asked Xiao Li, "Why do you think this data is fluctuating?" He was a bit nervous at first, but then he slowly began to analyze it. In the end, he derived the conclusion himself. The result was much better than if I had just told him.

After the meeting, several young engineers came to me on their own initiative to ask questions. Their attitude was noticeably different. It wasn't the defensive stance you see after being criticized; they genuinely wanted to understand the principles.

## Reflections

This method takes more time. If I had followed my old efficiency standards, I could have finished in five minutes what took twenty today. But the outcome was different.

Looking back, I’ve spent all these years chasing "correctness" and "efficiency." Point out the problem, give the answer, and move on to the next. But perhaps the issue isn't the answer itself, but whether they truly understand the "why."

I’m thinking that maybe this is what I should leave behind for this team before I retire—not just a complete operating manual, but a way of thinking. A dedication to detail and an attitude of not being afraid to ask why.

I used to think these young people weren't serious enough, but looking at it now, perhaps it was my approach that gave them no opportunity to truly think.

I’ll continue to observe the effects tomorrow. I might try this approach again during next week’s process audit meeting.

---

**Notes**: The defect rate remained at 0.8% this week, continuing to stay below the industry average.
