# MEMO

**Date: Saturday Morning, October 22, 2022**

Had a phone call with Chen Hao. After hearing about my diagnosis, his first instinct was to ask if I’d been taking my medicine on time and if he should take time off to come back. I didn't expect him to ask that.

Before this, I always felt like there was something standing between us. Our calls were always about work, the house, or whether they were eating well—like going through a checklist. Their answers were always brief. I thought that was just how we were.

But this time was different. When he said, "Dad, you need to take care of yourself," I heard something different in his voice. My own voice choked up a bit; all I could manage was, "Okay, thank you." That’s rare for me.

Li Meifang says the way I’ve shown concern for the kids all these years has been flawed—always supervising, never just being there. I used to disagree with her. But thinking about it now, maybe she’s right.

**A few things I need to figure out:**

1. Have a real talk with Chen Hao and Chen Yu individually before I retire. Not to ask about their careers or lives, but to truly listen to them. Maybe they have things they want to say to me, too.

2. What does "legacy" actually mean? I’ve always wanted to pass down my understanding of work and my obsession with quality to them. But they have their own lives. Maybe I should just record these things for them to reference, rather than expecting them to inherit it all.

3. The collaboration with the technical school—donating tools, doing sharing sessions. I want to do this even more now. Not to prove anything, but because I truly want to leave these things behind.

4. Have a proper talk with Li Meifang next week. She’s been by my side all these years, yet I’ve always been "managing" her. It’s time to change that.

**One more thought:**

Maybe the things I’ve done right over these forty years aren't the process improvements or the defect rate data. Maybe it’s that obsession with detail, that commitment to responsibility, and that care for my family—even if I haven't been great at expressing it. If Chen Hao and Chen Yu can see these things in me, that’s enough.

I don’t need them to become me. I just need them to know that I’ve always been here.

---

*Going to play chess with the guys this afternoon. My game hasn't been great lately; my mind keeps wandering. But maybe that’s okay—I don’t need to win. Just sitting there, being with them, is enough.*

# June 15, 2023, 6:45 PM

Sitting in the car, waiting for the light to change. My phone is on the passenger seat; all three messages have been sent.

I don’t know why I sent them. The entire afternoon was a blur of farewells—the handshakes, the photos, the looks in those young engineers' eyes, and that intern with her eyes red from crying. It all just sat heavy in my chest. It wasn't until I started driving that it hit me: I’ve never spoken to them like that before.

To Chen Hao, I wrote: "I haven't been there for you enough over the years because I was too busy with work. I hope we can talk more in the days ahead. Don't stress too much about the mortgage; we'll figure it out together."

To Chen Yu: "You are educating students, and I am manufacturing products; we are both pursuing excellence. I am proud of you. It’s my regret that I haven't truly listened to you talk about your work all these years."

And to Li Meifang: "Thank you for being by my side all this time. I know I’m not good with words, but you’ve always understood. From now on, I want to spend more time with you."

The moment I hit send, I regretted it. It felt a bit overly sentimental. But I didn't delete them.

Forty years. From that green kid fresh out of college to the man sitting here now. I always believed my value lay in the data—defect rates, process improvements, every perfect standard. This afternoon, a young engineer told me he decided to enter this industry because he read my paper, *Deviation Control in Precision Machining*. I didn't know how to react at the time.

Maybe... maybe what I’m leaving behind is more than just those things.

I don't know what to think right now. The light is still red. The phone hasn't budged. This feeling of waiting is unfamiliar—it’s not waiting for a work result, but waiting for... what? Their response? Or waiting for myself to adapt to this new identity?

I don't have to wake up at 5:30 tomorrow. That thought should make me happy, but right now, I mostly feel a sense of blankness. Like a precision part suddenly removed from a machine, unsure of what use it has left.

But... maybe that’s not a bad thing.

Maybe this is the beginning.

# Monday, September 12, 2022 – Sunny

After the morning meeting, I sat in my office, my hands still trembling slightly.

It wasn’t nerves. It was because I had just done something that didn't feel like me.

Li Ming, my subordinate, messed up his data sheet again—the third time this month. By my standards, a low-level error like that should have been called out directly; I should have made him understand exactly what "unacceptable" means. I had even rehearsed the blunt words in my head. But in the conference room, when I saw his tense expression, I paused.

I paused.

What I actually said was: "Let’s look at how we can improve this together." Then I spent ten minutes explaining it to him, instead of my usual three-sentence reprimand.

It’s so strange.

Sitting here now in the corner of my office, I’m trying to understand why I did that. Is it because I’ve been dwelling on the doctor’s words all week? Or is it because I’m starting to realize that I might actually be changing?

The doctor said my body is failing. Pre-metabolic syndrome. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. A blood sugar level of 110 mg/dL. These numbers are like nails driven into my brain. I always thought that through precise management—routine, control, supervision—I could maintain everything. But the doctor used data to tell me that I’ve failed.

Maybe that’s why I softened my stance with Li Ming. Maybe it’s because I suddenly realized I can’t control everything. I can’t even control my own body.

So, what right do I have to treat my subordinates that way?

The thought feels foreign to me. It also feels frightening.

Because if I let go of my obsession with detail, if I abandon that relentless management style, then who am I? What was the point of everything I’ve stood for over the last forty years?

On the other hand, I saw a spark in Li Ming’s eyes after I changed my tone. He started listening intently, started asking questions, instead of just hanging his head and taking the criticism. Maybe this change... maybe it isn't entirely a bad thing?

I don’t know.

I’m exhausted right now. These thoughts have drained my energy. The pressure is still there, but it feels different. It’s no longer anger at imperfection; it’s anxiety about time. How much longer? How much time do I have left to change? How much time is left to pass things on?

My wife said yesterday, "I knew you’d have this eventually." She was right. She’s always right. But I didn’t want to admit it.

Now, I have to.

The medicine the doctor prescribed is still in the drawer. I haven't taken it yet.

Tomorrow. I’ll deal with it tomorrow.

# July 12, 2023, 7:00 PM

Today was a bit unusual.

During dinner, Meifang brought up the idea of going to Yunnan; she said she wanted to see Erhai Lake. My first instinct was that old habit of mine—mentally calculating the cost of flights, hotels, and the itinerary, weighing whether a trip at this time of year would be "worth it." But I stopped. I didn't let the thought go any further.

I asked her how long she wanted to go for. She said she didn't know, maybe she just wanted to wander slowly without having to rush. As I listened, it occurred to me how I’ve planned my trips all these years—every minute had to be maximized, every cent precisely accounted for. Why?

I told her, "Then let's not have a tight schedule. We can stay in one place for two or three days and just take it in slowly." The moment the words left my mouth, I was a bit surprised myself. Coming from me, they sounded foreign.

Meifang’s eyes welled up a little. She said she had been waiting a long time to hear me say that.

I didn't answer. I just reached out and took her hand. We sat there like that for a while in silence. Her hand was a bit cold, a familiar touch. In thirty-five years, moments like this haven't happened often.

As I put down my chopsticks, I realized I was doing something I had never done before—I was letting go of the need to calculate everything. It’s not that I don’t care anymore; it’s that I suddenly understand that some things simply cannot be calculated.

Maybe this is what the doctor wanted me to realize. It’s not about giving up the pursuit of quality, but about understanding that life itself doesn't require so much precision.

I’ll keep looking up travel information tomorrow. But this time, I’ll let Meifang help make the decisions.

Perhaps this is the way I was meant to learn.

Don't forget to go to the hospital tomorrow morning to get your prescription filled. You have to take that blood pressure medication exactly as the doctor said—no skipping doses.

I looked over your recent check-up results, and your blood pressure is still a bit high. Starting today, you need to cooperate too. We’re extending your morning walk to 40 minutes, and I’ll be going with you. Also, less salt in our dinners; I’ll pick up some low-sodium salt this week.

And stop eating those pickled foods. I’ve made a list and sent a photo to you—just follow it when you’re grocery shopping.

Let’s go for a stroll in the park this weekend, and while we're at it, we'll head to the hospital to register for a full physical. No need to wait until the end of the year; it’s better to get checked now.

Don't find me naggy. I have to look out for your health.

June 19, 2023, 8:15 PM

Another night like this.

I just sent Hao a WeChat message, asking if he’d considered adjusting his investment strategy, given how the interest rate environment is shifting. He replied with a simple "Okay, Dad," and that was it. I stared at the screen; those two words felt like a wall.

I know I’ve fallen into my old habits again. I clearly wanted to show him I care, to hear how he’s been lately, but the moment I opened my mouth, out came advice. Just like back at the department—pointing out the problem directly, no preamble. My wife used to tell me I was like this; I didn't take it seriously then, but now I finally understand what she meant.

I opened my finance app and scrolled through my portfolio. The numbers are clear; every transaction is accounted for. Seeing the figures in the account grow gives me a sense of security. At least these things are still within my control. Unlike... unlike my conversations with Hao. Unlike the identity I’m about to lose.

At the farewell gathering before my retirement, an intern’s eyes welled up as he told me that my words had changed his understanding of precision machining. I didn't say anything at the time; I just nodded. In that moment, I felt I still had value. But what about now? I can’t even have a proper conversation with my own son.

I’m starting to think that maybe I haven’t changed at all. Maybe all those thoughts about "embracing vulnerability" were just me lying to myself. I still habitually want to control, to guide, to solve problems through perfect logic. But people aren't like numbers; they don't operate according to the parameters I set.

Hao is in Shenzhen now, facing heavy mortgage pressure and job instability. I care about these things, so I want to help. But how do I help? Through a cold piece of advice?

I opened his WeChat again, wanting to say something more, but then I put it down. I don't know what to say. This feeling is unfamiliar, and it hurts.

I have to go back to the hospital for a follow-up tomorrow. My chest has been feeling a bit tight lately—it might be psychological. Or maybe it isn't.

Forget it. I’m going to sleep.

# June 28, 2022, 10:15 PM

The light in the workshop is still on. Li Meifang has already gone to bed. Her blood pressure has been a bit high lately, and she’s still not really drinking the herbal tea I brewed for her. Forget it, I’ll deal with it tomorrow.

I just flipped through my notebook again. I’ve been documenting the tools since last year, and there are over thirty entries now. For each one, I’ve noted its purpose, specifications, and date of purchase. Some of the handwriting has started to fade, so I traced over it again in pencil.

A question suddenly crossed my mind—if my body starts to fail, what will I have left to do?

This question has been circling in my head for a long time. The May physical exam report is still in the drawer; I haven’t looked at it a second time. I remember all the metrics and indicators the doctor mentioned, but I chose not to dwell on them. And now? I still don't want to think too deeply about it, but it seems I can’t avoid it anymore.

My eldest son is in Shenzhen, preoccupied with his mortgage; my daughter is teaching in Guangzhou. I’ve never asked them if they’re interested in learning any of this. Perhaps they lost interest long ago. Perhaps I shouldn't have expected anything from the start.

I added another question mark under the one already in the notebook. Then, I placed the notebook in the most prominent spot on the workbench. I kept staring at it in the darkness. It’s a bit of a foolish gesture, but I just wanted to look at it. I wanted to see if those questions sitting there meant they truly existed, and truly needed to be resolved.

*While I can still move, I have to hurry.* This phrase keeps popping up now.

I proposed four more process improvement plans at work this year, and all of them were adopted. My subordinates are still afraid of me. A new QC inspector made three mistakes last week that I pointed out in front of everyone; now he’s all submissive whenever he sees me. I know it’s not right, but I can’t help myself. Maybe I’m using this as a way to fight back against something—against time, against powerlessness, against that growing realization: I am losing control over everything.

Li Meifang says I’ve become even quieter lately. She’s right. What else is there to say?

Tomorrow, I’ll continue documenting the tools. There are still seven or eight left to write down.

Must hurry.

July 10, 2022, 10:47 PM

I pulled out this unused notebook, though I’m not sure why. Maybe it’s because what happened yesterday is still spinning in my head.

I lost my temper yesterday over a spreadsheet formatting issue. Xiao Wang had reversed the order of the data columns, and I pointed it out in front of everyone, using some pretty harsh words. His face went pale instantly. I saw it, but I didn’t stop. Instead, I kept going on and on about how "details determine product quality, and product quality determines the company's survival."

Thinking back now, what was I so angry about? A formatting error. It was that simple.

But it wasn’t, really.

I’ve noticed myself setting stricter standards lately. Stricter inspection processes. Stricter requirements. As if doing this could... could what? I can’t quite put my finger on it. Maybe I’m trying to prove something. Maybe I’m trying to control something.

Li Meifang asked me what was wrong last night, but I didn't answer. She’s long since used to it.

I’ve gone over my May medical report three times now. The doctor said there’s nothing majorly wrong, just that I need to "pay attention." Pay attention to what? Pay attention to time passing. Pay attention to my body changing. Pay attention to how many more years I can keep doing this job.

I reorganized the tools in the workshop again recently. I’m recording the purpose of each one, one by one. Chen Hao won’t use them. Neither will Chen Yu. They have their own lives. I know this, yet I’m still taking notes. Why? While I can still move?

How many years of movement do I have left?

I’m supposed to play chess with my friends next week. I don’t know what to say to them. Lately, when I’m with them, I’ve been pathetically quiet. I was always a man of few words, but now I feel like I can’t even focus on the game. My mind is always elsewhere.

I’m trying to fight something off by exerting more control. I know this. But I can’t stop.

Xiao Wang didn't look at me today. I should say something to him, but I don't know what.

Maybe I’ll delete these words tomorrow. There’s no need to leave this behind.

But for now, I’ll let them stay here.

October 24, 2022, Evening

I’m sitting in the study; it’s pitch black outside. My wife is in the living room watching TV, the volume turned down low. I haven’t turned on the lights—I’m just sitting here.

Something happened at the chess room today that I can’t stop thinking about.

This afternoon, while we were playing, Friend A casually asked how I was doing, mentioning that I didn’t look great. I was going to brush it off, but for some reason, I just said it—the doctor’s diagnosis. I said it calmly, as if I were talking about the most ordinary thing in the world.

They didn’t react the way I expected. There was no pity, none of that "Oh, you must take care of yourself" tone. Friend B just nodded and said, "Then you’d better take your medicine and stop staying up late." Friend C even gave a small laugh and said, "At our age, who doesn’t have something wrong with them?" Then they went back to the game.

That was it. It was so natural. It was as if I had simply told them the weather wasn't great.

Sitting there, I suddenly felt a strange sense of relief. It wasn't because their response was particularly warm, but because they didn't treat me like someone who needed sympathy. They still saw me as a fellow player—just an ordinary person who gets sick and grows old. That’s all.

I’ve realized that for all these years, I’ve been doing one thing: trying to trade perfection for worth. I had to be the best at work, keep my health in check, keep the household in perfect order—I even felt the need to bluntly point out every mistake my subordinates made, as if that was the only way to prove my value.

But their reaction today made me see that maybe I don't need to do that.

I think about how my wife has been lately. She hasn’t asked about my diagnosis again, nor has she shown any unusual worry. She just cooks as usual, reminds me to take my meds as usual, and sits by my side as usual. Sometimes she talks about family trivia, and I just listen. This kind of companionship feels more comfortable than my old way of "monitoring her blood pressure and pushing her to get checkups."

I used to call that "caring." Now I’m not so sure. Maybe it was just control.

At dinner tonight, I took the initiative to ask Hao Hao how he’s been. He seemed a bit surprised, but he answered me seriously. I didn't ask about his mortgage pressure like I usually do; instead, I asked if he was happy at work. He said he was doing okay. I said, "That’s good."

That was it. No more words. But it felt different.

I don’t know how long this feeling will last. But tonight, sitting in this dark study, I feel as though I’ve let go of something. It’s not giving up—it’s finally admitting that I, too, am allowed to be fragile.

Maybe this isn't failure. Maybe this is just what it means to be alive.

Another formatting error cropped up in QA this morning. It wasn't a major issue, but I can't stand that kind of slackness when it comes to details. I was right there on the floor and called it out immediately; my voice might have been a bit loud. I told Meifang that I need to overhaul the department’s standard operating procedures. Just emphasizing it verbally isn't enough; it has to be documented with every step clearly defined. As she listened, she just looked at me that way, and I knew what she was thinking: *Here we go again.* But this time is different. I can feel that if things continue like this, the problems are only going to multiply.

She said, "Don't push yourself too hard." I didn't respond to that. After a few bites of food, I explained once more why these standards must be strictly enforced. She just nodded and kept eating.

The truth is, I know my subordinates are afraid of me. But what’s wrong with a little fear? Fear makes people diligent. I’ve been in this position for 12 years, and our defect rate is 25% lower than the industry average—that’s not because I have a pleasant temperament.

Lately, I just feel this constant urge to hurry. Whether it’s work or anything else. While I’m still able to move, I need to get everything done right.

After returning home that evening, Chen Guilin changed his clothes and sat down at the dining table. Li Meifang was serving the soup.

He ate a few mouthfuls of rice in silence before putting down his chopsticks.

"I might have been too harsh at the department meeting today," he said, his voice low, eyes fixed on his bowl. "The new QC inspector messed up the data entry again, and I called him out in front of everyone. He looked pretty shaken."

Li Meifang looked up at him but didn't respond immediately.

"My temper has been short lately," he paused, tapping his finger on the table. "I can feel it myself. I have high standards for the work, but..." He didn't finish the sentence, opting for a sip of soup instead.

He turned to his wife, his tone tinged with uncertainty. "Do you think I'm being too demanding? I want to get things done right, but it seems like..." He hesitated, "it seems like I always end up making people uncomfortable."

He wasn't looking for comfort; it was more like he was trying to confirm something. There was exhaustion in his eyes, along with a certain guilt he couldn't quite put into words.

"The physical exam is next week," he suddenly changed the subject, though his tone remained heavy. "I need to get to bed early these next few days."

He lowered his head and continued eating, but his movements were much slower than usual.

March 20, 2022, 11:47 PM

Sitting in the workshop. My wife is already asleep. The noises downstairs have all quieted down.

I finished page thirty today. Starting from the first page, my handwriting was neat; the purpose, specifications, and purchase date of every tool were recorded clearly. But toward the end, the pen started to shake. I’m not sure when it began—maybe around page twenty-five. The strokes became irregular, and some words looked rushed.

When I got to the entry for the wrench, I stopped for a long time. I bought this wrench in 1998. It’s a Japanese import with a precision of 0.02 mm. I’ve used it for over twenty years, and it shows very little wear. I wanted to record its use, but the pen just hovered there. Then I wrote—"Who will ever use this?"

After writing that sentence, I didn't cross it out. It’s strange. I’m not usually like this. The notebooks on my desk have always been spotless—no scribbles, no traces of hesitation. But this time, looking at that sentence, I just couldn't bring myself to erase it.

Hao is in Shenzhen; Yu is in Guangzhou. Neither of them will ever come to this workshop. Hao is in real estate sales, and Yu teaches. They have their own lives. I never expected them to inherit anything, but sitting here now, looking at these tools, I suddenly realized—these things are about to become useless. Not because they’re broken or lost, but because no one will know how to use them.

This morning, Meifang asked me what I was busy with. I only replied with a grunt. She didn't ask again. She’s used to my silence. After thirty-five years, she’s long known that I’m a man of few words. But when that "mm-hmm" came out today, even I could hear it—it wasn't silence; it was avoidance. I don’t know how to explain to her what I’m doing. I can’t even explain it to myself.

The physical exam report is still in the drawer. My blood sugar is up a bit again. The doctor said I need to be careful. I’m in my fifties now; my body is starting to disobey me. I used to think that as long as I kept things under control and followed the plan, nothing would go wrong. But the body doesn't follow a plan. Neither does time.

I’m a little over a year away from retirement. I’ve been waiting for this day, but now that it’s almost here, I’m starting to feel afraid. Afraid of what I’ll do after I retire. Afraid of being idle. Afraid that these tools will just keep sitting here, unused and misunderstood.

The notebook is still open. "Who will ever use this" is still there on page thirty. Tomorrow, I have to keep writing. There are still many tools left to record. I have to get them all down while I can still move.

But even if I record them, what then?

# September 15, 2022, 19:30

Sitting in the study, my hands are still shaking a little.

But when the words reached my lips, I didn't say them. Instead, I heard myself say, "I’m nervous too."

The moment those words came out, even I was a bit stunned.

I never say things like that in front of her. For forty years, I’ve been the one in control—the accounts, the plans, every matter in the house, big or small. She’s used to my composure, and I’m used to being the one who must remain unshakable. But today, I admitted it. I said, "Let’s go together."

And then, I took her hand.

Thinking back on that gesture now, it still feels strange. My hand holding hers; her hand was very warm, probably because she was nervous too. We just sat there for a while, neither of us speaking. I could feel her looking at me, but I didn't look up.

The doctor’s terminology is still echoing in my mind—metabolic syndrome, atherosclerosis, long-term medication. I always thought that through discipline and control, I could manage my body like a precision machine. But now I realize I have no control at all. The body has its own logic; it doesn't run according to my schedule.

The moment I held her hand, I wasn't thinking about the diagnosis itself, but rather—if I really collapse, what happens to her? All those tools, forty years of experience, the things I haven't had time to organize yet...

I don't know if this counts as weakness. I’ve always hated weakness. But today, it seems I allowed myself a little bit of it.

This isn't just a simple transfer of knowledge. I’ve realized that every decision I made and every improvement I implemented in this role can actually serve as a reference for others. Perhaps this is the contribution I am meant to make.

### II. Precision Tool Donation Project
The tool list I compiled last week has been preliminarily categorized. There are 42 items in total, including:
- Precision vernier calipers (8 units)
- Micrometers (12 units)
- Various specialized measuring gauges (15 units)
- Tool maintenance supplies (7 units)

Yesterday, I found three more old tools in exceptionally good condition—they date back to my father’s era. There’s no point in letting them sit idle at home; it’s better to let the students put them to use. Li Meifang said, "Your father would be happy up in heaven." I didn't say much, but I took her words to heart.

The donation ceremony is tentatively scheduled to coincide with the lecture. The school will establish a "Precision Tool Display Room," and my name will be engraved on a plaque. To be honest, this isn't about leaving a legacy. I just feel that these tools being used and cherished is far more meaningful than having them sit in my house.

### III. Conversation with Chess Friends
I was originally supposed to play chess with Friends A, B, and C at the club tonight. This afternoon, I had a sudden thought to treat this gathering as a small "summary meeting."

I told Friend A about my recent plans—the lecture, the donation, and my post-retirement arrangements. Their reactions were unexpected. Friend B said, "You’re doing something very difficult—letting go." I didn't respond immediately, but that sentence has been spinning in my head ever since.

It’s true; over the years, I’ve grown accustomed to control—controlling quality standards, controlling workflows, even quietly controlling my family’s medical checkups and diets. But I understand now that I cannot control everything. I can only choose how to live. The lecture, the donation, the communication with my children, the travel plans with my wife—these are all my choices.

Friend C joked, "You’re performing a 'quality improvement' on your own life." We all laughed. But the metaphor is actually quite accurate.

### IV. Family Communication
I called Chen Hao last night. This time, I didn't just ask about his mortgage and work; instead, I asked what he’s been busy with lately and what’s been bothering him. He seemed a bit surprised but chatted for a while longer than usual. He said the mortgage pressure is indeed heavy, but things are looking up with a few big clients recently. I listened and didn't rush to give advice; I just listened.

I sent a WeChat message to Chen Yu asking if she has time to come home during the summer break. She replied that she might be able to visit in early August. I discussed it with Li Meifang, and we’d like to take a short trip nearby when she returns. Meifang said it was a great idea and even offered to plan the route in advance. This is the kind of conversation we haven't had in a long time.

### V. Work Handover Preparation
Although I still have over a year until my official retirement, I’ve started organizing key work documents and procedural manuals. This isn't out of a rush to leave, but to ensure nothing is missed during the handover. My successor (or rather, the current deputy manager) needs a clear understanding of these materials.

Today, I organized two important files: *The Evolution of Quality Inspection Standards* and *Guidelines for Handling Common Defects*. These are the accumulation of my years of work. While writing, I thought to myself: this knowledge shouldn't retire with me. It should stay behind.

---

## Reflection

Sitting in the office this afternoon organizing these things, a thought suddenly struck me—what have I been doing with my life? Precision work, managing teams, financial planning, maintaining order. These are all important. But I am beginning to understand that they aren't everything.

The passing on of knowledge and experience can happen through a lecture, a tool donation, a conversation with my children, or even through casual chat while traveling with my wife. My daughter is teaching, my son is building, and I am manufacturing—we are each in our own positions, in our own ways, passing on the pursuit of excellence.

That is enough.

---

**Plan for Next Week**:
- Continue organizing lecture materials
- Confirm the specific time for the donation ceremony with the school
- Prepare for the final inspection and inventory of the tools
- Discuss the early August travel route with Li Meifang

**Notes**: Energy levels are good. Stress has eased. Continue proceeding as planned.

Are you asleep?

I was just in the study tidying up my tools, thinking about what you said. You’re right—I really have changed.

For years, I always felt that being responsible meant keeping everything under control—with you, the kids, and my work. I only realized later that it wasn't responsibility; it was a lack of trust. I didn't trust that you could take care of yourself, or that they could make their own decisions.

It wasn't until that incident with the doctor that I truly understood—there are some things I simply cannot control. Through it all, you were the one who stayed, never once looking down on me.

Thank you for being by my side.

Let’s take things slow, together.

October 16, 2022, Evening

It’s been two hours since I got back from the hospital. Sitting here in the study, I still find myself somewhat at a loss for words.

The results from this morning’s check-up were steadier than expected. The doctor said the medication is working well and my heart rate is stabilizing. I had prepared a mountain of data and questions to ask, but with Meifang sitting beside me, I suddenly changed my mind. I heard myself say, "We are both cooperating with the treatment"—a strange choice of words coming from me. In the past, I would have said, "I am strictly managing it," or "I am controlling my diet." But today was different.

As I held my wife’s hand, I realized what I’ve been doing to her all these years. I’ve been "monitoring" her—monitoring her blood pressure, her diet, her physical exams. It was as if she were a quality control metric for me to manage. Yet she never once resisted. She was just there, by my side.

The doctor suggested joining a health management course. My first instinct was to refuse—too busy with work, not enough time. But I looked at Meifang and didn't say no right away. I asked her, "What do you think?" Those four words felt foreign coming out of my mouth. She said we could give it a try. And so, it was decided.

I wonder if this is what they call "vulnerability." Admitting that I can’t control everything, that I can’t stop my body from aging, and that I can’t ensure every plan stays on schedule. It’s not a comfortable feeling. But it’s not as terrifying as I imagined, either.

Perhaps all these years, I’ve been using perfect self-discipline to prove my worth. Now, I’m starting to wonder: was it worth it?

Meifang is downstairs preparing dinner. I can hear the sounds from the kitchen. It’s quiet, ordinary. But today, it sounds different.

I haven’t quite figured out what all of this means yet. But at the very least, I’m no longer in such a hurry.

# Work Log

**Date:** Wednesday, October 5, 2022
**Time:** 14:20
**Recorded by:** Chen Guilin

---

## Incident Record

**Participants:** Xiao Wang (QC Team Leader)
**Location:** Production Workshop Inspection Area
**Category:** Management Communication / Process Optimization

**Background:**
During the 2:00 PM workshop inspection, I noticed abnormal fluctuations in the dimensional measurement data for Line 3. Three products from the morning batch exceeded the tolerance range by 0.02mm, which is unacceptable. Normally, I would have pointed this out directly as a low-level mistake. But I paused.

**Process:**
Instead of criticizing him directly as I usually do, I asked Xiao Wang, "What’s your take on this fluctuation?"
He stammered, suggesting it might be a calibration issue with the testing equipment.
I replied, "That’s possible. But let’s look at it together and see if there are other ways to interpret this."

We reviewed the data logs from the past week together. It turned out that the new probe replaced last Friday had a slight deviation; because the error was so small, it had been overlooked. I asked him, "If that’s the cause, how do you think we should improve the process to catch this kind of issue earlier?"

After thinking it over, he proposed an idea: every time testing equipment is replaced, we should perform three consecutive validations using standard parts instead of the current single check. It was actually a solid suggestion.

**Result:**
Xiao Wang’s engagement increased significantly. He took the initiative to propose an improvement plan rather than waiting for my orders. We documented this new inspection process together and ran a trial this afternoon. It worked—the data for subsequent batches stabilized.

**Reflection:**
This coaching approach seems more effective than direct criticism. The subordinate not only corrected the problem but also participated in designing the solution. This might be a management optimization worth scaling up.

---

**Tags:** #CommunicationOptimization #ProcessImprovement #DataQuality

**Next Steps:** Observe the applicability of this new inspection process on other production lines. Consider promoting this method during next week’s departmental meeting.

October 30, 2022, 8:00 PM

Li Meifang is already asleep. I’m sitting in the study; on the desk lies today’s medical report and a cup of tea that went cold long ago.

I said something to her today that left even me a bit stunned. I said, "Perhaps the meaning of life isn't in perfect control, but in genuine connection." Hearing those words come out of my mouth felt like someone was impersonating me. And yet, it really is what I was thinking.

Since I started the medication these past few days, the physical side effects haven't been as bad as I imagined. The doctor says it’s a good sign. But what’s stranger is that I’ve actually started to relax. I used to check my medical reports three times a day, recording every single number in a notebook, trying to force them back to "normal" through sheer willpower. Now... I still look at the reports, but I no longer feel the need to "subdue" them.

Last night, Meifang took my hand. She does this often, and I used to pull away after a moment to get back to my accounts or the news. But this time, I didn't. I let her hold it—maybe for five or six seconds. She didn’t say anything, and neither did I. We just held hands. Eventually, she let go gently and went to the kitchen to pour me some water.

I realize now that I owe her a great deal. For thirty-five years, I’ve used excuses like "monitoring her blood pressure" or "managing the family finances" to package my need for control as "responsibility." In truth, it was just my way of proving I was useful, that I deserved to be needed—by controlling everything. She saw through it long ago, but she never said a word. She just stayed by my side, waiting.

Chen Hao called recently saying his mortgage is a heavy burden. In the past, I would have immediately started analyzing his finances, giving him advice, even feeling a bit dissatisfied with his "poor planning." This time, I just listened, then told him, "It’s okay, take your time." There was a pause on the other end of the line; he didn’t seem used to me responding like that. I’m not used to it either. But it felt... more real.

Then there’s that idea about donating my tools to the technical school. Initially, I thought of it as "legacy"—wanting young people to learn what I’ve accumulated over forty years. But today I realized that was just another form of control; I wanted their learning to prove that my value wouldn't vanish with retirement. I still want to donate them, but the reason has changed. I just want to put those tools where they can continue to be used and cherished. As for who uses them or what they learn, that’s not for me to decide, nor does it need to be.

It’s strange. I always thought letting go of control would make me panic. But actually, it makes me feel... light?

My subordinates at the office are still a bit afraid of me. I know that. But lately, I’ve been thinking that maybe I could try a different way. Not by lowering standards, but... by changing how I communicate. Letting them know my expectations are high because I believe they can do well, not because I’m disappointed in them. This will require me to say more, and I’ve never been good at that. But perhaps it’s worth learning.

Tomorrow is Monday. Another week begins. I’ll keep taking the medicine, keep working, and keep being there for Meifang. And maybe, at the right moment, I’ll sit with her—saying nothing, just holding her hand.

That is enough.

Meifang, I just gave the Industrial Technical School a call.

I’ve been thinking about it for days, and I’ve decided to donate the tools in my workshop. Not everything, but most of the precision tools and measuring instruments. The school said they could set up a practical training base for the students to use.

I was thinking, these things are just sitting here at home. Instead of letting them grow old with me, it’s better to put them to use. I graduated from that school back then, so I suppose you could call this giving back.

I’ve been thinking a lot lately. Maybe I’ve been waiting for something, or someone, to inherit these things. But waiting like this is pointless. It’s better to take the initiative and do something.

I want to clear out the workshop next month and sort through what can be donated. I might need your help with the packing. I’ll make a list, noting the purpose and maintenance for each tool, and send it to the school along with the donation.

Thinking about it this way, I actually feel more at peace.

What do you think?

September 12, 2022, Evening

Sitting in the workshop, wiping down my bench vise. My movements are slow, rubbing the same spot over and over.

I read this afternoon’s medical report three times. Blood sugar at 110 mg/dL, Stage 2 hypertension, and that term—pre-metabolic syndrome. The doctor spoke calmly, but those numbers are driven into my brain like nails. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. Long-term medication.

I don’t understand.

For the past forty years, I’ve never been careless. A regular routine, annual checkups, a controlled diet—I even managed Li Meifang’s blood pressure. I thought this was how you stayed in control. I always believed that precise management could stave off decline. But the doctor told me—it’s not enough.

My wife said something in the kitchen earlier that I’m still turning over in my mind: "I knew you’d have this sooner or later."

She’s right. That makes it harder to swallow.

I agreed to her suggestion that we go to the hospital together. I just said "Okay." She probably didn't notice my hands were shaking.

After dinner, I asked her how to be a better mentor to my subordinates. She looked startled for a moment, then started talking about things at her school. I was listening, but my mind was running a different set of numbers—how much longer? How many working years are left? Those tools, that experience—do I still have time to pass them on?

It’s too late. The thought surfaced suddenly, crystal clear.

I don’t want to admit that I can’t control everything. But I can’t deny that report, either. The doctor told me to pick up my prescription tomorrow. Long-term medication. The phrase feels stuck in my throat.

It’s 7:45 PM. Li Meifang is watching TV. I’m in here, wiping my tools.

I need to make a new plan. But right now, I don’t know where to start.

# March 28, 2022, 9:45 PM

Had a phone call with Haozi.

He said business has been pretty good lately, and the mortgage pressure isn't as heavy anymore. I originally wanted to ask him if he had any interest in mechanical engineering—if he wanted to know more about what I’ve been working on all these years... but the words died in my throat. I changed the subject and asked how work was going instead. He said it was fine, then asked if there was something I wanted to say.

I told him it was nothing, that everything was under control.

After hanging up, I stood in the living room for a long while, phone still in hand.

I thought about the tools in my workshop. Forty years. Some I modified myself; others I repaired after they broke. Every single one has a story, but those stories only exist in my head. I’ve recorded some of it in my notebooks, but what’s the point of recording things? Haozi won't read them. Yuyu won't read them either. They have their own lives, which is only natural.

But I still feel... wrong.

These things, this knowledge—it just ends here. I’ve been in this position for twelve years, proposing several improvements every year that the company adopted. But those will end, too. When I retire and the new manager arrives, he’ll have his own methods. The optimizations I made might eventually just become a statistic—"defect rate down by 25%"—and nothing more.

Li Meifang is downstairs watching TV. I didn't go down.

I pulled out the last physical exam report and read it again. Blood pressure is a bit high. The doctor said to be careful. Mine has started to fluctuate, too. It was stable for the last two years, but now...

I can't control it.

That’s what hurts the most. I can control workflows, quality standards, and my subordinates' performance. But I can't control time. I can't control my body. I can't control whether Haozi or Yuyu will inherit anything.

In the margin of one page in my notebook, I wrote: "Who will ever use this?" I didn't erase it.

I have to go to the office tomorrow. There’s a quality control meeting in the afternoon. A new employee’s report isn't detailed enough; I need to point it out. It’s my responsibility. But I’m getting a bit tired of this kind of thing. Or rather... I know where this frustration is coming from. It’s because I’m demanding perfection from others while I myself am losing my grip on it.

Thinking this way makes me feel a bit hypocritical.

Forget it. Time for bed. There’s a lot to do tomorrow.

I need to hurry while I can still move.

# Memo

**Date:** July 12, 2023, 7:00 PM

**Subject:** A thought regarding our travel plans

---

Today, while discussing retirement travel arrangements with Meifang, I said something: "We don't need a frantic itinerary. We could stay in one place for two or three days and just take it slow."

After I said it, I paused, surprised at myself.

That doesn't sound like me. At least, not the old me.

I was always the one whose schedules had to be precise to the hour, whose trips had to maximize sightseeing, and whose efficiency had to be peak. Wasting two or three days in one spot? That sounded like a waste of one's life. That is how I have always thought.

But now I am wondering if I have misunderstood something.

Lately, I’ve been spending more time talking with Meifang. She talks about family matters, our daughter’s recent work, and the neighbor’s kids. In the past, I would listen while my mind drifted to the work at hand. Now, sometimes I put everything down and just sit there listening to her. Sometimes no response is needed; it’s just about being there. That feeling—I finally understand now—is not a waste of time.

It’s the same with chess. A weekly game with my friends can sometimes last three or four hours. There is small talk, there is silence, and there are those long pauses for thought. I used to think of it as my only form of relaxation. Now I think it might be life itself.

So why should travel be any different?

If we stay in a small town for two or three days—not rushing, not checking off landmarks, but just sitting somewhere having tea, watching how the locals live, or watching nothing at all and just being by Meifang’s side—that isn't "low efficiency." This might be the very thing I’ve been chasing but never found.

I spent 40 years optimizing production processes and eliminating waste. That was right—it was right for the factory. But life is not an assembly line.

Perhaps quality is not about density, but about depth.

I need to remember this thought. After I retire, I might forget, or I might start planning the old way again. So, I am writing it down.

---

*Note: Tomorrow, look for some suitable places with Meifang. Let her choose.*

July 8, 2023   Evening

After the game ended, I sat alone in the car for a long time before driving home.

What Player B said kept circling in my head: "From taking to giving." I didn't react immediately at the time; I just kept arranging the pieces. But thinking back now, those words were like a needle, pricking something deep inside.

I always thought my post-retirement anxiety came from losing my professional identity. But today I realized that might not be it. Maybe what I’m truly afraid of is that I have nothing left to give. When I was working, I had defect rate data, process improvement plans, and a relentless obsession with quality. Those things made me feel valuable. And now?

This afternoon, I took the initiative and said I’d bring out my tool collection to show them. The moment the words left my mouth, I was surprised at myself. It’s not like me. But Player A immediately said "Great," and Player B nodded too. They seemed like they actually wanted to see them. It made me feel… I don’t know how to put it, just a bit different.

I’m thinking that maybe my value didn't lie in those professional achievements. Maybe it lies in these tools, in the things I can share with others. I can tell them the purpose of every single tool, how to maintain them, and how to use them to make a piece of work more refined. That knowledge didn't vanish just because I left the job.

But at the same time, I’m doubting myself. Is this a real realization, or am I just lying to myself again? I’m trying to find a new identity, a new sense of worth. Maybe this is just my old habit—control. I can’t control work anymore, so now I’m trying to regain a sense of mastery by sharing tools, by "giving."

I’m a bit tired. My energy is low. But that moment this afternoon, when I invited them over, I felt something different. It wasn't that sense of accomplishment you get from work, but… a sense of being needed? Or rather, a sense of connection with others.

I need to think about it more. Maybe I can invite them over next week. I’ll need to tidy up the workshop.

Also, I’ve realized that my recent "supervision" of Meifang has probably gone too far. She gave me that look again today. I know she’s rejecting my concern. But I can't let go. I’m still trying to prove I’m useful through control. This habit is really hard to break.

Anyway, that’s enough for now. I’ll think more tomorrow.

Played some chess over the weekend. A chess friend mentioned how bad the market is lately—houses just aren't selling. It made me think of the pressure Haozi is under in Shenzhen; I’m a bit worried for him.

The weather was nice, but I’ve really felt my physical stamina declining these past two years. After three games of chess, I felt quite tired. Checked my blood pressure when I got home; luckily, it’s still within the normal range.

Spent some time watching TV with Lao Li this evening. She’s been controlling her diet lately and is sticking to it well. She has a follow-up appointment at the hospital tomorrow.

Gave the tools in the workshop another wipe-down. They’ve been sitting there for quite a few years now. Sometimes I wonder what will become of these things later on. But for now, I should just focus on getting the tasks at hand done.

Need to make the most of things while I’m still mobile.

July 5, 2022, 6:45 PM

Had a call with Hao. He said the real estate market has been a bit cold lately, but he’s holding on. I asked him if he’d ever thought about learning something about mechanics—maybe it could be a hobby later on. He laughed and said, "Dad, what are you talking about? I don’t have the brain for that." I didn't push it further.

After hanging up, I sat in the workshop for a long time.

Actually, that wasn't what I wanted to ask. What I wanted to ask was—has he ever thought about what will happen to all these tools, all these things I’ve spent forty years learning? But I couldn't get the words out. Just like I never can.

During our call this afternoon, I told him "everything is under control." Thinking back on that phrase now, it feels a bit ridiculous. What does "under control" even mean? What am I controlling? Hao is in Shenzhen worrying about his mortgage, Yu is busy with work in Guangzhou, and here I am, documenting the uses of these tools as if I’m in a race against time. But time isn't racing me—it doesn't care about me at all.

Li Meifang is downstairs watching TV. I can hear her coughing. It’s time to nag her about going to the hospital to check her blood pressure again. These are things I can control. But what’s the use of controlling them?

The tool records in my notebook are halfway finished. With every entry I write, I wonder—who is going to use this? Hao? No. Yu? Even less likely. So why am I writing all this?

Maybe it’s just to keep myself from being idle. To tell myself there’s still something left to do.

The physical exam report from May is still in the drawer. A few of the indicators fluctuated a bit. The doctor said it’s nothing serious, just needs regular follow-ups. But I keep taking it out, looking at those numbers over and over. As if I could find some hidden meaning in them.

I’ve never known how to talk to them. That’s the truth. It’s been that way since they were little. I thought it would get better as they grew up, but it seems to have gotten worse. Now, I even self-censor before asking a simple question, terrified of saying something that might cause awkwardness.

Maybe I should have started teaching them earlier. But back then, I was busy with work. Now that I want to teach, it’s already too late.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue documenting the tools. There are still over thirty items left to record.

I should do it while I can still move.

# September 5, 2022, 8:15 PM

I deleted that spreadsheet today.

It took me a long time to finally hit the delete key. My finger hovered over the mouse for nearly five minutes. That spreadsheet held three months of Meifang’s blood pressure readings, diet logs, exercise durations... I had color-coded everything: green for 'target met,' yellow for 'needs improvement,' and red for 'danger.'

When I finally clicked it, I realized I was sweating.

I said those words to Meifang tonight. She was in the kitchen, and I stood in the doorway and said, "My over-supervision isn't protecting you; it's hurting you." She turned around and looked at me with something in her eyes I can't quite describe. She didn't say anything, she just nodded.

We were silent for a long time. Then she said, "We are living together, not just surviving in fear every day."

When I heard that, I felt something in my chest loosen.

Afterward, I sat in my workshop for two hours. I didn't organize my tools; I didn't do anything at all. I just sat there. I looked at those precision parts and instruments, wondering where they would eventually end up. Maybe I really should donate them to a school. Maybe that’s the right thing to do.

I’ve made a new plan. Not one of those extreme ones filled with numbers and color codes, but something... more like a plan for living:

Exercise three times a week—not for the data, but for the feeling of being alive.
Check medical data once a month, not every week.
Eat healthily, but not to extremes.
See a therapist next month. I need to deal with the reality of aging. The reality of death.

I can't figure these things out on my own.

The medical report is still on the desk. I haven't looked at it again. Maybe I’ll look tomorrow, maybe the day after. It’s okay not to look right now.

Back to work tomorrow. Back to chess next week. Life goes on.

But it feels different now.

July 25, 2022, 10:45 PM

I’ve been sitting in the workshop for two hours. Haven’t moved.

While I was organizing my tools this afternoon, I came across that notebook. The one from last year. Tucked inside was a slip of paper with my own handwriting on it: "Who is ever going to use this?" I don’t remember writing it. When I saw those words, my hands started to shake.

I massaged my temples. I still haven’t quite recovered.

Forty years. From entering the factory in 1982 until now—forty years. Every single one of these tools has a story. That Swiss precision knife? Bought it in 1995 during a business trip to Beijing. That set of measuring gauges? I’ve used them for over thirty years, and the precision is still within 0.01 millimeters. I can tell you the origin, the purpose, and the maintenance routine for every piece here. Sometimes I spend my weekends here, wiping them down one by one, documenting them one by one.

But—who is going to use them?

Hao is selling real estate in Shenzhen. Yu is teaching in Guangzhou. They’ve never asked me about these things. I never expected them to. But sitting here now, I suddenly wonder: I spent all this time maintaining, organizing, and recording... for what? In the end, is it all just a pile of unwanted junk?

I donated some tools to the technical school. They were very happy to have them. But that’s different. That’s a donation, not a legacy. A legacy should be someone standing by your side, watching how you use the tools, asking why you do it that way, and then carrying it on. Right now, no one is asking.

Yesterday’s physical exam results aren't out yet. My blood pressure is high again. The doctor said I have to keep it under control. I know I do. I control everything every day—recording my wife’s diet, my own step count, every single data point. It’s as if by recording things meticulously enough, I can stop something from happening. But it’s a lie. I know that in my heart.

My hands are shaking again.

Forty years of work might end up as a pile of scrap metal. Or sold as secondhand junk. Or tossed into a recycling bin. When I picture this in my head, I find it a bit hard to breathe.

Maybe I should just donate it all. Let the students at the school use them. At least they’d be useful. It’s better than leaving them here for me to look at every weekend—a constant reminder of what I once did and how I can’t change anything now.

Li Meifang is downstairs watching TV. She doesn’t know what I’m thinking up here. She never asks. I’m used to not saying anything.

I need to hurry. While I can still move. But hurry for what?

—

The notebook is closed. I didn’t write any more.

September 10, 2022, 8:15 PM

I’ve been sitting in the workshop for two hours. I’m wiping down that 1985 vernier caliper, my movements slow. My wife is watching TV in the living room; I can hear the sound, but I don’t want to go out.

110 mg/dL. Stage 2 hypertension. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis.

The doctor spoke those words calmly, as if reading a product inspection report. But I heard them clearly. Every word felt like a nail being driven in. I’ve always done my checkups, always logged the data, always believed I was in control. Wake up at 6:00 AM, sleep at 10:00 PM, a light diet, regular exercise. For forty years, that’s how I’ve lived.

My wife said, "I knew you’d get this sooner or later."

That sentence stung more than the diagnosis itself. When did she see through me? I thought I’d hidden it well. I thought that through precise management, I could stop aging, stop my body from betraying me. But she knew all along. What does that mean? Does it mean my efforts look like a joke to everyone else?

Long-term medication. The doctor said I need long-term medication.

I don’t know how long "long-term" is. One year? Five? Ten? Until I die? I’ve never taken medicine long-term. I rarely even catch a cold. Now I have to take pills for the rest of my life just to maintain a number—110. The number itself is meaningless, but what it represents matters. It represents my loss of control over my own body.

My wife said she’d go with me to get the prescription filled tomorrow. I didn’t respond, just nodded, and turned to walk into this workshop. I didn’t want her to see my hands shaking.

The tools are still here. Dozens of them. I spent forty years collecting them. Each one represents a stage, a decision, a moment I thought was important. Now I’m wondering, if I collapse, what happens to these things? Chen Hao doesn't understand mechanics. Neither does Chen Yu. Will they be treated as scrap metal?

I donated some tools to the industrial technical school. Was that the right decision? Or was it just a disguised way of settling my estate?

I don’t want to think like this. But I can’t help it.

The doctor said I need to change my lifestyle. Reduce stress. Maintain a routine. I’m already doing those things. Does that mean I haven’t done enough? Or does it mean none of it matters?

My hands are still shaking. I put the caliper down.

I have to get the medicine tomorrow. I need to make a list—drug names, usage, side effects, prices, insurance coverage. I need to turn this into data, into something manageable. That’s the only way I’ll feel at peace.

But I know that some things simply cannot be managed.

May 22, 2023, 10:47 PM

Sitting in the study, my journal is still open on the desk. It’s a habit now, writing every day. But tonight, the words are coming slowly.

The farewell party ended this afternoon. I stood there, looking at all those young faces. When they applauded, I didn’t feel much; it just felt a bit surreal—forty years, just like that, over.

But that intern’s tears... I’m still thinking about that. She said, "I’ve never heard a talk like that before." I don’t know if I said anything particularly special. I just spoke about some details from work, about how to handle a difficult quality issue. I also talked about... about how angry I was back then, and how I criticized a subordinate in the harshest way possible.

I paused for a long time before saying, "Maybe there was a better way."

It felt strange saying those words. Like I was admitting to something. In forty years, I’ve never said anything like that in front of a crowd. I always felt that admitting to imperfection was the same as admitting to failure. But today... maybe it’s not.

After the session, those young engineers came up to discuss technical details. They asked a lot of questions. I answered them. There wasn't that tension of hierarchy; it was just... discussing a problem. A real problem.

I’m thinking now that knowledge doesn’t necessarily have to be passed down in a specific way. It doesn’t have to be my son inheriting my craft, or forcing someone to learn something. Maybe it just happens naturally—I speak, they listen, they ask, I answer. Just like that.

Tomorrow, I leave that office for good. I’ve sat in that chair for twelve years. The folders, pens, tools on the desk... they all need to be organized and left behind. I wonder, what am I leaving behind?

Not objects. Something... else.

Maybe it’s just those stories. The ones I thought were nothing special.

My wife asked me how I felt today. I said I was fine. But honestly, I don’t know how to put it into words. Relief? Unease? A bit of both. There’s no work schedule for tomorrow. How am I going to arrange my days? I’ve thought about this for a long time, but I still don’t have an answer.

Maybe I need to stop chasing the perfect plan. Maybe... maybe I should just let things happen naturally.

Like the tears of those young people.

I’m still getting used to the idea.

I’ll think about it tomorrow.

3:30 AM. Awake again.

The photo of that prescription is still glowing on my phone. I don’t know why I keep looking at it over and over. The numbers are the same every time: blood pressure 140/110, fasting blood glucose 110 mg/dL, LDL-C 3.8 mmol/L, carotid intima-media thickness... The doctor used the term "pre-metabolic syndrome." And "long-term medication."

Long-term.

I shone a flashlight on the paper prescription, reading it word by word. The doctor’s handwriting is clear. I didn't misread it. I copied the data into my notebook again, just like I would verify a test report at work. Confirmed. It’s ironic.

How much longer can I keep working?

My hand was shaking as I wrote that sentence. Then I crossed it out. I wrote it again. Crossed it out again. In the end, I just sat there, staring at the blackened ink.

Forty years. From the day I entered the factory in 1982 until now, every day has gone according to plan. Every process precise to the millimeter. The defect rate in the department I manage is 25% lower than the industry average. I have never wasted a single minute. Regular diet, regular sleep, annual check-ups. I thought that was enough. I thought I was in control.

But the doctor said it wasn't enough.

Meifang’s words keep circling in my head: "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." She said it so calmly, like she was stating a fact she’d known all along. I didn’t argue. Because I realized I couldn't find the words to talk back. She saw right through me. She saw through forty years of self-deception.

I don’t believe this result. But I can’t deny it either.

There are four medications on the prescription. I haven't filled them yet. I sit in my office, looking at those precision tools, imagining myself taking these pills every day, my body slowly...

How many years are left?

I tried to draft a plan in my notebook. Retirement date, financial arrangements, things for the children. But I couldn't keep writing. Because suddenly, I don't know what the deadline for these plans is anymore.

Dawn is breaking. I should get up. But I don’t want to move.

I think I need to recalculate everything. But right now, I don’t have the strength.

— Chen Guilin
September 11, 2022, 3:30 AM

You’re right. Those snacks are your choice, and I shouldn't always be telling you what to do. I’ve realized that lately I’ve been overstepping—interfering with your diet, your checkups, everything. That’s not caring; it’s controlling.

I know what I’m doing. Since I lost my job, I’ve brought that whole mindset home with me. I needed to feel like I was still in control of something. But it’s not fair—it’s not fair to you.

I trust you to take care of your own health. You know your body better than I do. I’m going to change.

What do you want for dinner tonight? I’ll go pick up some groceries.

# Work Log

**Date:** May 22, 2023  
**Weather:** Sunny  
**Recorded by:** Chen Guilin  

---

## Summary of Events

From 14:00 to 16:30, the "Quality Management Experience Sharing Session" was held in Conference Room B. A total of 23 people attended, including 12 employees from our department and 11 young engineers from the Engineering and Production Departments.

## Content Shared

1. **Topic:** Key Milestones and Process Optimization in Forty Years of Quality Management Practice
2. **Case Studies Covered:**
   - Process improvements for reducing product defect rates in 2008 (involving 3 production stages)
   - Establishment of the supplier quality management system in 2015
   - Updates to inspection standards for precision components in 2020 (reducing error by 0.3%)
3. **Reference Materials:** Work log excerpts, process documentation, and inspection data comparison tables

## Post-Session Feedback

The following interactions took place after the presentation:

- An intern from the Engineering Department (name not recorded) took the initiative to ask about the criteria for precision testing; the discussion lasted about 15 minutes.
- A technician from the Production Department raised two questions regarding process details, which were answered on the spot.
- Two employees from our department requested the relevant documents; I have promised to send them out next week.

**Observation:** The engagement level of the young engineers was higher than expected. Their questions focused on specific work scenarios rather than theoretical concepts. This suggests that the effective transfer of knowledge depends not only on the completeness of the content but also on the speaker's ability to establish a connection with the audience through real-world cases.

## Personal Reflection

Forty years of work ultimately distilled into a single afternoon of sharing. The numbers are clear—23 people, 3 cases, a 15-minute discussion. But I am still reflecting on the things that exist beyond those figures.

I noticed the look in that intern’s eyes. She took notes very diligently; her voice was quiet when she asked questions, but they were very pointed. It reminded me of my younger self. Perhaps this is what "passing the torch" truly means—it isn't a forced indoctrination, but rather a moment where someone is willing to listen intently, and someone is willing to speak from the heart.

## Follow-up Recommendations

Consider establishing a more systematic mechanism for knowledge transfer:
1. Build a technical document library to archive experiential case studies.
2. Launch regular technical salons (suggested once a month).
3. Explore the feasibility of a mentorship program to bridge the gap between experience and practice.

These initiatives can be moved forward by my successor.

July 11, 2023, Night

Today, I finally did something I’ve been thinking about for a long time but never got around to—labeling my tools.

After returning from the office this afternoon, I spent two hours in my small workshop. I picked up every single tool, wiped it clean, and used a fine-tip pen to write down its origin and purpose on a label; for some, I even noted the year I bought them. That vernier caliper from 1995, that set of precision screwdrivers I brought back from a business trip to Japan in 2003... halfway through, I realized what I was actually doing—I was telling stories for these objects.

Meifang came in and asked what I was up to, so I told her. She didn't tease me; instead, she sat down beside me and listened to the story behind every tool. I had never shared the details of my work with her like this before, but today I just kept talking—slowly and meticulously.

When Chen Yu called, I happened to be talking about that pair of lineman’s pliers. I kept her on the line and continued. She listened intently, and later she said, "Dad, you are shaping a pursuit of excellence." I was a bit stunned when I heard that. She said she wanted to integrate these stories of mine into her teaching, to let her students know that work isn't just work—it’s a reflection of how a person thinks and gets things done.

I didn't know what to say, so I just said, "Okay."

When she came over later, we sat in the workshop for a while longer. She gave me a hug. My body was a bit stiff at first, but then I relaxed. I gently patted her back, just like... just like I should have done a long time ago but never did.

I understand now. I’ve been worried that my knowledge would have no heir, fearing that I’d lose my value after retirement. But that’s not the case. Chen Yu is teaching students in her classroom, Chen Hao is building things in Shenzhen, and I am here making quality products. Each of us, in our own place, is passing on this dedication to doing things right. That is enough.

That is enough.

I’m sitting at my desk now, looking at those labeled tools, and things feel a bit different. They are no longer just my collection; they are a conversation. A conversation between me and Chen Yu, and perhaps, a conversation between me and the students who will eventually receive these tools.

Maybe retirement isn't the end. Maybe it’s a different kind of beginning.

# After dinner, in the bedroom

Meifang, the school called today.

The one I donated the tools to. They said they’d like me to give a guest lecture to the students—something about precision tools and craftsmanship.

I told them I’d give it a shot.

(A pause)

To be honest, I’m not sure how well I’ll do. I’ve spent all these years working in the factory; I haven't really spoken in front of that many students before. But they sounded very sincere, saying the kids are genuinely interested in this field.

What do you think? Was I too hasty in saying yes?

My head is a bit of a mess right now. Part of me feels like this might be a good opportunity, but another part feels like I might be overestimating myself. Then again... if I don't try, I'll never know if I can do it.

(Another short pause)

How about we brainstorm an outline after dinner? I’d really like to hear your thoughts. Where do you think would be a good place for me to start?

## Morning Work Records

- 08:30-11:00 Inspected the quality control process in Production Workshop No. 3. Found three discrepancies in the parameter records for the welding process; immediate rectification has been requested.
- 11:15 Conducted a feedback session with Inspector Xiao Wang.

## Feedback Details

Xiao Wang missed two Class B defects in yesterday’s inspection report. In the past, I would have directly pointed this out as an unacceptable dereliction of duty. But today, I paused and asked him why.

He said those two defects were in hidden locations, and he had hesitated, ultimately not feeling confident enough to confirm them. Instead of reprimanding him, I examined the samples with him, explained the judging criteria, and told him that next time he encounters an uncertainty, he should consult me or other senior inspectors.

His demeanor visibly relaxed. In fact, he took the initiative to suggest whether we should work together to develop a "Judgment Guide for Ambiguous Defects."

It’s a good suggestion. I used to think employees should figure these things out on their own, but thinking about it now, perhaps it was my approach that made them afraid to speak up.

## Afternoon Work Records

- 14:00-16:30 Worked with Xiao Wang to compile fifteen common cases of ambiguous defects and established a preliminary judgment reference library.
- 16:45 Department meeting. Reported this month's defect rate: 0.8%, a 0.2% decrease month-on-month.

## Reflections

I noticed a trend this month: employees have submitted 5 more suggestions for work improvement compared to last month. There have been no new resignation letters (there were 2 last month).

I’m starting to think that perhaps my old "harsh to the point of being ruthless" management style did lower the defect rate, but it also suppressed people's initiative. I’ve only been changing my approach for two months; the data remains good, but the atmosphere is different.

An idea: Could I put together a "Quality Inspection Experience Summary" for technical school students before the end of the year? Not for some grand sense of "passing the torch," but just to document what I’ve learned over the years—including the failures.

Tomorrow, I will continue pushing forward with the parameter optimization for the welding process.

# Memo - Reflection and Legacy

**Date: October 18, 2022, Afternoon**
**Location: Home Study**

---

## Core Points Summary

### I. Reflections on the Essence of 40 Years of Work
- ~~Perfect quality control~~ → Obsession with detail is a value in itself
- ~~Sense of achievement from process optimization~~ → The patience and persistence learned through the process
- A 25% drop in defect rates is not the finish line, but a reminder: Why do I demand so much?
  - Answer: Because I believe in standards.
  - The real answer: Because I am afraid of losing control.

### II. Beyond the Technical (Important)
- The meaning of tools lies not in collecting them, but in how they taught me to think.
- What precision work taught me:
  - A single millimeter determines success or failure.
  - However, the distance between people cannot be measured in millimeters.
  - (Thinking back on my harshness toward subordinates and my "supervision" of my wife over the years.)
- I must admit: I cannot control everything.
  - The doctor's words are still ringing in my ears.
  - The body ages; plans change.
  - This is not failure.

### III. If I Could Start Over
- Explain to the team *why* we do things earlier, rather than just telling them *what* to do.
- Give young engineers more room to make mistakes (instead of blunt dismissals).
- ~~Keeping knowledge in my head~~ → Actively record, share, and discuss.
- Listen more to my wife, rather than "supervising" from the sidelines.
- Communicate with the children about more than just their mortgages and jobs.

### IV. Words for Young Engineers
- Standards are important, but don't let them become a weapon.
- Details determine quality, but details can also consume your life—learn to prioritize.
- 40 years is a long time, but it is also very short.
- The things you take for granted now will become mere memories by the time you reach my age.
- Don't wait for your heart to remind you—some things are more important than perfection.

### V. Immediate Action Plan
- [ ] Compile a "Core Experiences in Quality Management" document (for technical school students).
- [ ] Sit down with my subordinates for a talk—explain rather than accuse.
- [ ] Have a real conversation with my wife this weekend; listen to her talk about home.
- [ ] Call Chen Hao and Chen Yu respectively; do not mention work.
- [ ] Continue medication and scheduled check-ups—accept this reality.

---

## Notes
- The precision tool inventory can be set aside for now.
- The 40-year timeline is drawn; now it needs to be turned into a story.
- Perhaps retirement isn't the end, but a different kind of beginning?
- (I wouldn't have believed that sentence before.)

---

**One final question: What is my greatest regret in life?**
It isn't that I didn't reach a higher position.
It’s that I never truly looked at the people beside me.

# Workshop Notebook

**August 21, 2022 – Evening**

I’m sitting in the workshop, still holding that pair of pliers. Meifang is downstairs; she’s probably clearing the dinner table. I didn’t go down to help her, just like always.

But today is different.

During dinner, she said something. I can still hear her voice—"We are living together, not so we can spend every day in fear." Her eyes were red, but she didn't cry; she just looked at me.

I didn’t know how to respond. I never do.

For these past 35 years, I thought I was protecting her. Monitoring her diet, her blood pressure, her medical checkups... I thought that was love. But sitting beside her, I suddenly understood that I’ve actually been torturing her. My fear—my fear of loss—I’ve been crushing her under the weight of all of it.

I am afraid of losing her. For the first time, I’ve admitted it this bluntly. Not through actions, not through control, but by actually saying the words: "I’m just afraid of losing you."

She cried. We sat there for a long, long time, saying nothing.

I don’t know what this has changed. Or if it changed anything at all. My body is still aging, the medical reports are still lying there, I’m still retiring in two years, and I still don’t know what I’ll do after I retire. None of that has changed.

But something has shattered. That wall I spent 40 years building. The thing I built out of indifference, control, precise data, and plans. It’s broken now, and I’m standing among the shards, feeling naked and powerless.

I cannot stop time by recording it. I cannot guarantee safety through control. I cannot prove my worth through perfection.

I’m starting to wonder what I’ve been doing all these years.

The tools in the workshop are still there. Dozens of them, arranged neatly. No one will ever use them. My children won't. Maybe I should donate them. Maybe that’s where they belong.

I don’t know where I belong.

It’s 7:30 PM. Meifang is probably downstairs waiting for me. I should go down. But I’m sitting here, gripping these pliers, not wanting to move.

I’m afraid that if I go down, I’ll see her face. Afraid that I’ll retreat into silence again. Afraid that everything will go back to the way it was.

But I’m also afraid that it won’t.

I don’t know which is more terrifying.
