June 29, 2023  Sunny

I didn’t sleep well last night. I was still wide awake at 2:00 AM, my mind looping over those tools.

When Li Meifang listened to my stories, there was something different in her eyes. She sat on the sofa, listening intently as I told her about the vernier caliper I bought in 1985. Even I was a bit surprised—I’ve never spoken to her about these things in such detail. That caliper has been with me for over thirty years; it’s accurate to 0.02mm, and it still works. Then there’s the set of screwdrivers imported from Japan in 1995—the wear on the handles is a record of my years of work.

I told her that perhaps what matters more than professional achievements is being able to pass this experience on to the younger generation. As the words left my mouth, I felt a flicker of surprise. It didn’t sound like me.

She took my hand. I didn’t pull away.

That gesture made me pause for a long time. We’ve been married for thirty-five years, and I’ve rarely been the one to initiate such things. Usually, she reaches out, and I… accept it. But last night was different. When she took my hand, I didn’t let go immediately; instead, I squeezed back.

Sitting in my office now, I’m wondering what that moment meant.

I’ve always sought security through control. Controlling workflows, controlling quality standards, controlling her diet and medical checkups… I’ve been aware of this for a while, but I only truly felt it last night. When I let go of that tension and just sat beside her telling stories, I felt a sense of… I don’t know how to describe it. It wasn’t exactly relaxation; it was more like… being permitted.

I’m still thinking about that question: without my professional identity, who am I?

Maybe I’m someone who can pass on experience. Maybe I’m Li Meifang’s husband—a husband in the true sense of the word, not just the one providing financial security. Maybe… I’m still figuring it out.

But at least last night, the moment I held her hand, I felt something. It wasn’t an answer, but perhaps it was a direction.

The tools will be donated to the school. That decision no longer feels empty.

# October 1, 2022, 6:45 PM

Just got back from a walk with Meifang. The same old route—from the front door to the park and back, about thirty minutes.

I’ve been thinking about what she said. "I knew this day would come for you. I’ve been waiting for you to admit that you, too, will grow old, and you, too, will get sick."

When did she see it? I keep wondering. Maybe she saw it a long time ago. Maybe from the moment I started monitoring her blood pressure and her diet, she saw right through me—that I was actually afraid. I always thought I was caring for her, but in reality... I was controlling. Controlling her in the name of care.

This realization is hard to swallow.

During our walk, I told her, "I’ve been monitoring you all this time, but I never once asked how you felt." She didn’t answer immediately; she just kept walking. After a while, she said, "It’s okay. I’m used to it." That sentence hurt more than any accusation could.

I wonder if I’ve been like this for the past forty years. Being blunt with my subordinates at work, and doing the same with my wife and children at home—demanding, managing, and dismissing them under the guise of "it’s for your own good." My eldest son’s mortgage pressure, my daughter’s marriage—I’ve been paying attention to all of it, but I’ve never truly asked them what they want.

Lately, I’ve been obsessed with this health management plan. Logging blood pressure, recording meals, drafting exercise routines. I told myself that as long as I managed it well enough, I could reverse the doctor’s diagnosis. But during the walk today, it hit me—maybe I’m not managing my health; I’m in denial. Using data and schedules to deny that diagnosis.

She said, "you, too, will grow old, and you, too, will get sick." I’ve never wanted to admit that. I thought that if I were disciplined enough, precise enough, I could be the exception. But maybe there are no exceptions.

At dinner, she was the one who asked if I wanted to go for another walk. I said yes. For the first time, she was the one asking me, rather than me finding excuses to refuse.

I haven't told the children yet. What am I waiting for? Maybe I’m waiting until I’m ready to admit that this isn’t a problem that can be "managed" away.

Work tomorrow. Three process improvement plans to finish. Still so much to do.

But tonight, sitting here, none of that is on my mind.

September 10, 2022, 9:45 PM

I’ve been in the workshop for nearly three hours. Li Meifang knocked twice; I didn’t answer.

The morning’s medical report is still spinning in my head. Blood sugar at 110 mg/dL, Stage 2 hypertension, and that phrase—"pre-metabolic syndrome." The doctor spoke calmly, as if reading a production report, but I caught the "you should be worried" undertone. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. Long-term medication.

I’m sitting at the workbench, holding a pair of pliers, wiping them over and over with a piece of flannel. How many times have I wiped them? I’ve lost count. Usually, I follow a system—precision measuring tools in one group, cutting tools in another, fasteners in a third. Not today. I just pick up whatever is at hand, wipe it, put it back. Pick up another. My movements are slow because my hands are shaking slightly.

I don’t want to admit that.

Forty years. For forty years, I’ve been in control—schedules, diet logs, regular check-ups. My desk is organized without a single stray wire. The household ledgers are accurate to the penny. I thought that was enough. I thought that with enough precision, enough discipline, I could keep everything in my grasp.

But the doctor said it’s not enough.

That sentence keeps ringing in my ears—the one Li Meifang said: "I knew you’d get this sooner or later." When did she know? How did she know? Was she pitying me, or was she saying, "I saw through you long ago"? I don’t want to ask, because I’m afraid of the answer.

110 mg/dL. That number is wrong. Something has gone wrong with the management of my life, but I can’t find where. Or rather, I can’t find a solution at all. Pills? Long-term medication? What does that mean? It means my body has begun to betray me, and I cannot reverse it through stricter management.

How much longer?

The question has suddenly become urgent. How much longer can I work? How much longer until I see Chen Hao’s mortgage paid off? How much longer until I pass these tools, these forty years of things, on to someone? I never gave it serious thought because I assumed there was plenty of time.

Now, I’m not sure.

The tools are still here, piece by piece. My hands are still shaking.

I have to go to work tomorrow, read production reports, point out mistakes to my subordinates. Everything must continue. But I don’t know how much longer I can keep going.

I need to look at that report again. Maybe I missed something. Maybe the numbers will change if I scrutinize them enough.

But I know they won’t.

April 5, 2022. Night.

I’ve been sitting in the office for a long time. Wang Gang’s quality inspection report is still spinning in my head.

The formatting was off again when he handed it in this morning—the classification standards in the header didn't match last year’s specifications. It’s such a basic thing to get wrong. I couldn't help myself; I snapped at him. He tried to explain, but I cut him off. No excuses. Professionalism is about an obsession with detail.

His face changed the moment I said it.

Looking back now, my tone was harsh. Harsher than usual. He’s actually a decent employee. In the past, I would have pointed out these small mistakes, but never like that. Why today...?

My fingers have been tapping on the desk for a long while. Rapidly. I didn't even realize I was doing it.

What am I thinking? Was his report really that outrageous? Or... was I just taking it out on him?

It’s been like this lately. I’m becoming more demanding with my subordinates and more micro-managing with my wife. This morning, I was looking at her blood pressure logs again; a single high reading and I started feeling restless. I spent another whole night in the tool shed, numbering all the precision tools and documenting their uses. I’m terrified that one day I’ll suddenly... lose the ability to move, and these things will become completely useless because no one knows how to handle them.

Two years until retirement. Two years.

I know what I’m trying to do. I’m trying to control things. Control the work, the standards, everything at home—even the aging of my own body. It’s as if by managing every tiny detail, I can prove I’m still useful, that I can still... what?

Wang Gang has to come in for work tomorrow. I need to figure out how to handle this. Not for his sake, but because... I don’t want to become this kind of person. I don’t want to use harshness to mask my own fear.

But I don’t know how to change.

How much longer can I do this? How much longer can I keep things under control like this?

3. Contact the industrial technical school to ask how we can ensure these tools are actually put to use, rather than just sitting in a display case.

**An observation:** Li Meifang’s question actually helped me. It made me stop and re-examine what I’ve been doing. I realize I might have been too anxious lately—obsessing over health data and over-monitoring her diet, trying to use control to fill the void left by my lost professional identity.

Maybe I need a change of perspective. Instead of trying to control things, I should think about what I can pass on.

This isn't just a "pastime" for retirement; this is something meaningful.

# MEMO

**Date: September 19, 2022, Morning**
**Location: Office Cubicle**

---

Maybe I should change my ways.

The improvement plan Xiao Wang submitted during yesterday’s meeting was actually quite good. At the time, I pointed out three flaws directly. His expression… I can still see it now. He didn’t push back; he just nodded and took notes. When I walked past his desk at the end of the day, he was packing up—his movements were hurried, as if he were escaping something.

Is this right?

I’ve always believed that being strict is being responsible. A 25% reduction in defect rates—those are the facts. But what lies behind the data? It’s my subordinates working in fear under my gaze. Li Meifang once said, "You’re the same way at home." I didn't respond.

Control ≠ Respect.

Where did that phrase come from yesterday? Perhaps it was something the doctor said that triggered it. He talked about my blood pressure, my carotid artery, all those numbers… I’ve spent 40 years trying to control everything, yet my body has betrayed me anyway. I cannot control it. So what about my subordinates? I "manage" them the same way, as if I were managing a machine. But they aren't machines.

Time is running out. What actually matters?

It’s not the process optimization forms. It’s not those perfect quality inspection reports. If I were to collapse right now, who would remember how strict I was? Who would think of me because of a 25% lower defect rate?

Regarding Xiao Wang’s proposal—I actually should have praised him in front of everyone. And *then* pointed out the room for improvement.

I need to rethink how I speak to my staff.

Maybe there’s still time.

---

*To be continued*

# MEMO

**Date:** June 15, 2023, 6:45 PM  
**Subject:** Reimagining Continuity and Legacy

---

The farewell party ended this afternoon. Sitting in my office, looking at the empty desk, I found myself wondering: after forty years, what have I actually left behind?

I used to think the answer was clear: a defect rate 25% lower than the industry average, annual process improvement plans, and all those adopted proposals. These figures and achievements were proof enough of my value. But this afternoon, when that intern said with teary eyes, "Manager Chen, you taught me so much more than just technical skills," I suddenly realized I might have misunderstood everything.

Perhaps the act of passing things on is the purpose itself.

In the message I sent to Chen Yu, I wrote: "You are educating students, I am manufacturing products, and we are both pursuing excellence. I am proud of you." As I wrote those words, it finally clicked—I don't need her to inherit my job, nor do I need her to become another quality inspector. The fact that she pursues excellence in her own field is a form of continuity in itself. It’s not about bloodlines or obligation; it’s about everyone finding their own way to carry forward that obsession with precision and responsibility.

The tools will be donated to the school. This decision now feels like more than just disposing of old belongings; it’s another form of dialogue—letting those tools continue to serve a purpose in the hands of the young. I don’t need to see them become me; I only need to know that when they use those tools, they will remember that someone once spent an entire lifetime just to get things right.

The stories from the sharing session—how to spot a 0.02mm deviation, how to uphold standards on the assembly line, how to stay calm under pressure—these are not my intellectual property. They now belong to those who heard them. They will understand, improve, and spread them in their own way. That is enough.

I’m starting to feel that perhaps I’ve been defining "legacy" the wrong way. I thought a legacy had to be something visible, quantifiable, something that could be inherited in its entirety. But true continuity likely happens in those seemingly tiny moments—a sentence, a demonstration, a tool, a bit of persistence.

Starting tomorrow, I will no longer be the department manager. But that doesn't mean it's over. It’s just continuing in a different way.

This requires long-term reflection.

# Memo

**Date**: Wednesday, August 24, 2022, 14:00

**Subject**: Reflections following a game of chess

---

## Record

During today’s game with Player A, he said something that stuck with me: "Instead of spending time fearing the future, spend it cherishing the present."

I didn't respond at the time, but I haven't stopped thinking about it since.

## Issues to Sort Out

1. **What am I doing?**
   - My recent anxiety cycle: Prediction → Fear → Control → More Fear.
   - Since the physical exam results came back, I’ve been using data, plans, and logs to fight uncertainty.
   - But is this actually helpful? Or am I just wearing myself out?

2. **On Control**
   - What I can control: Workflows, financial accounts, daily schedules.
   - What I cannot control: Aging, fluctuating health metrics, my children’s choices, time itself.
   - I have been trying to fill the void of uncontrollable fears with things I can control.
   - What is the cost of doing this?

3. **On the Present**
   - For the past few months, I’ve been planning for a retirement that is two years away.
   - At the same time, I’m worrying about potential health issues.
   - The "present" I hold in my hands has been squeezed into something tiny.
   - My wife, the chess games, the tools in my workshop—they are all still here, but mentally, I’ve already let go of them.

4. **Defining Value**
   - I’ve always believed: Completion → Results → Proof of self-worth.
   - But what if things can’t be completed? What if the results are forgotten?
   - Perhaps value is more than just these things?

## For Further Reflection

- Why do I keep losing what I have now by worrying about what I might lose in the future?
- Is accepting the unchangeable an act of giving up, or an act of maturity?
- How much more time can I afford to waste on fear?

---

**Note**: When I play Player A again next week, I want to hear his take on "cherishing the present." Not as small talk, but because I truly want to understand.

# Work Log: October 30, 2022

**Sunday, 20:00**

I’ve been reflecting on something over the past few days. This afternoon, while sitting with Meifang for a long time, she mentioned that I’ve been talking a bit more lately. I just smiled and didn’t deny it.

Last Friday’s medical report showed that things have stabilized. The doctor said to just keep taking the medication on schedule and come in for regular check-ups. Meifang was right there beside me taking notes, even more focused than I was. It made me think back on all the "supervising" I’ve done at home over the years—checking her blood pressure, monitoring her diet. My intentions might have been right, but my approach was probably too rigid. She never complained; instead, she’s now taking the initiative to accompany me to the hospital. It’s a strange feeling, but a grounding one.

Back to the office tomorrow. I’ve been thinking about how to organize everything I’ve accumulated over these forty years before I retire. It’s not necessarily about passing it on to someone specific, but I feel there should be a record. On Monday, I plan to talk to my supervisor about putting together a summary of my quality management experience in the first half of next year, perhaps as a formal document. Also, regarding that technical school—I want to move forward with that. I plan to systematically categorize and label the precision tools in the workshop and donate them. In my hands, they are just tools; in the hands of students, they might become something more.

Feedback from my subordinates has been better lately. I’ve noticed that when giving feedback, I’ve started asking for their thoughts first instead of just pointing out problems. The defect rate remains low, but the atmosphere seems less tense. I haven't fully processed this shift myself, but it feels right.

Haohao called last week saying his mortgage pressure is still quite heavy. I didn’t jump in with advice immediately; instead, I asked what his plans were. He said he might need to adjust his work schedule. I told him, "As long as you’ve thought it through. Let us know if you need help." I surprised myself a bit saying that, but in that moment, I was being sincere.

Yuyu’s work in Guangzhou is also going well. She said she wants to come back for a visit after National Day; Meifang and I are both very happy about it.

Perhaps the meaning of life isn't about perfect control, but about genuine connection. That thought surfaced while I was chatting with Meifang—I didn't expect to say it myself. But once it was out there, it felt like I had finally admitted something.

Onward to next week.

June 15, 2022, 7:30 PM

I lost again at the chess room this afternoon. It wasn't that I lost to Player A; I lost to myself.

Player A said I played very conservatively today. The word he used was "cautious," but I could hear what he really meant—he was saying I’ve changed. I didn’t argue. I just said, "I’m getting older, I have to be more careful," and kept setting up the pieces. He didn’t say anything else.

But I know he saw it. I saw it in myself, too.

I used to dare to take risks, to make bold moves in the mid-game. And now? Now I pause and think for a long time before every single step. It’s not because I’ve become smarter; it’s because I’m afraid—afraid that one wrong move will lead to a point of no return. Afraid of wasting time.

It’s strange. A game only lasts two hours, and losing is just losing a game. But I find myself applying this same logic to everything in my life. I’m stricter with my subordinates at work, I’m more controlling over Meifang’s diet at home, and I even use a magnifying glass to scrutinize the numbers on my medical reports.

Why?

I tell myself this is being responsible. But deep down, I know it’s fear. Fear that time is running out. Fear that something is slipping through my fingers and I won’t be able to catch it in time.

The tools in the workshop are still there. The notebooks are there, too. I’ve recorded about half of it. Hao is in Shenzhen, and Yu is in Guangzhou. Neither of them has ever asked what those tools are for. I haven't brought it up, either.

Sometimes I wonder, living this cautiously—what exactly am I trying to protect?

I have to go back to the chess room tomorrow. I wonder if Player A will notice something else.

Forget it, stop thinking. Time for bed. I haven't finished going through the medical report from May; I’ll give it another careful look tomorrow.

It was already past 7 p.m. by the time I got home. My wife was busy in the kitchen; hearing the door, she poked her head out and asked if I’d eaten. I told her I hadn't, and she said the food was still warm.

I set down my briefcase and sat on the sofa. She brought over a bowl of soup. I took two sips and then just sat there, motionless.

As she was clearing the dishes, she asked, "What’s wrong? You seem a bit down today."

I didn't answer directly. After a moment, I finally said, "I didn't play very well today."

"Oh? Did you lose?" she asked.

"I didn't lose. It’s just... my reactions aren't as sharp anymore." I paused. "My chess partner said I was slow today. I felt it myself, too."

She sat down beside me. She didn't say anything, she just stayed there with me.

I spoke again: "Have you noticed anything about me lately?"

"Like what?" she asked.

"Just... feeling a bit tired." I rubbed my temples. "Maybe work has been too intense recently."

Actually, that wasn't what I wanted to say. What I wanted to ask was: Have you noticed me changing? Do you think I'm getting old? But I couldn't bring myself to say those words.

"Then get some rest early," she said. "I'll make you some red bean porridge; it's good for you."

I nodded. She got up and headed for the kitchen. I sat on the sofa, looking at my hands—the knuckles were a bit stiff, the skin becoming loose.

I took out my phone and opened last week’s physical exam report, looking over the data once more. My blood sugar was 0.3 higher than last year, and my blood pressure was fluctuating. I remembered what the doctor said, but I hadn't told my wife.

She was humming a song in the kitchen, her voice soft and gentle. It suddenly occurred to me that her blood pressure had been rising too. We are both changing; we are both aging. The thought made me feel a dull ache.

I turned off the screen, leaned back against the sofa, and closed my eyes.

I still have to go to the office tomorrow. There are still those tools, those records. Time is moving on, and it feels like there is less and less I can do.

Meifang, I’ve been thinking about something.

Just now in the workshop, I dug out those tools. Some I bought over twenty years ago; others I only recently organized. As I was wiping them down, it suddenly hit me—maybe what I’ve been doing all this time isn't about passing them on perfectly to someone else, but that there’s meaning in the process itself.

You told me before that I’d eventually have this realization. You saw it clearly. And now, I’m starting to see things clearly too.

I’m thinking that maybe, before I retire, I should talk to the younger ones about what I’ve learned over the years. Not to leave behind some kind of legacy, but just... to talk. To let them know why precision work is worth taking seriously.

I don’t know if this is the right idea. But I feel like I need to do something, while there’s still time.

What do you think?

# Management Methodology Update - October 19, 2022

**Subject: Blame vs. Coaching: A Comparative Analysis of Effectiveness**

While chatting with Meifang tonight about recent departmental affairs, I had a sudden realization.

**My old approach:** Employee makes a mistake → Point it out directly → State the standard → End of conversation.
**The result:** Low defect rates, but high turnover. Employees worked in constant fear, and their first instinct when a problem arose was to hide it.

**Changes over the last three months:** Employee makes a mistake → Ask "why" first → Analyze the cause together → Explain the correct procedure → Give them a chance to fix it.
**The result:** Xiao Li, who used to make frequent mistakes, hasn't repeated the same error in a month. More importantly, he has started proactively reporting issues instead of concealing them.

I told Meifang, "The moment I stopped blaming him, he actually became more willing to learn."

As I said those words, I even surprised myself.

**Systematic Improvements Required:**

1.  **Trigger**: When an employee makes a work error
    *   **Old Model**: Direct identification of the flaw + Criticism
    *   **New Model**: Understand the context first + Joint analysis + Coaching

2.  **Replicable Steps**:
    *   **Step 1**: Ask "What happened this time?" (Inquiry, not interrogation)
    *   **Step 2**: Listen to their explanation to find the root cause (could be unclear processes, faulty tools, or a misunderstanding)
    *   **Step 3**: Explain the correct method and the logic behind it
    *   **Step 4**: Have them summarize it back to ensure full understanding

3.  **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)**:
    *   Recurrence rate of similar errors (Target: Reduce by over 50%)
    *   Frequency of proactive issue reporting (Target: Increase)
    *   Employee turnover rate (Target: Stabilize or decrease)

**Reflection**:
For forty years, I have been too accustomed to measuring others against a "standard of perfection." Perhaps that is why my subordinates fear me, and perhaps it is also why I find my own life so exhausting.

I want to implement this methodology across the entire department before I retire. Not to please anyone, but simply because it is more effective.

**Note**: This can be shared at next week’s departmental meeting as a "New Approach to Quality Management."

September 22, 2022, 8:30 PM

I’m sitting in my workshop, still holding that pair of pliers. I’ve wiped them over and over, but they never seem to get quite clean.

The doctor’s words are still circling in my head. 110, atherosclerosis, long-term medication. The numbers are clear. My whole life, I’ve been best at dealing with clear numbers, but this time, that clarity feels suffocating.

Meifang said something yesterday that I hadn’t been able to put into words: "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." She saw it coming. In the office, I can spot a flaw in the smallest detail, yet I couldn’t see my own body slowly breaking down. Irony.

I pulled out that notebook. Usually, I only record work logs, accounts, and process improvements. Today, for some reason, I started writing something else. I wrote a sentence, crossed it out. Wrote it again.

"If I could do it over, I would teach the younger ones more."

My hand shook a little as I wrote that.

Forty years. Forty years in this factory, from inspector to manager. Those precision tools, those processes, those improvement plans I puzzled out all by myself… suddenly I wonder, what happens to all of it if I collapse? Will my subordinates keep up my standards? Or will everything just fall apart?

But then I thought, maybe I’m wrong. Maybe the value of these things doesn’t depend on me handing them over personally. Maybe it’s already there—in the processes I improved, in the standards I demanded, in the things those young people learned even while they were resenting me.

I wrote another line in the notebook: "Maybe I should hold a technical sharing session before I retire."

As I finished the sentence, the tightness in my chest loosened a bit. Not entirely, but it was there.

It’s not about proving anything. It’s just… if I talk about these things, if I let people see *why* I do what I do instead of just how strict I am, then maybe… maybe there won’t be so much regret.

The doctor said I need to control my diet, get regular check-ups, and take my medicine. I can do that. Following a plan is what I do best.

But this time, there’s something new in the plan. Not just to live longer—though I hope for that, too. But to make these forty years actually mean something.

Meifang is calling me for dinner from downstairs. I didn’t answer right away. I looked again at those sentences in the notebook, the ones I’d revised over and over.

Maybe I’ll propose the idea to my supervisor tomorrow. Maybe I won’t. I don’t know yet.

But at least I wrote it down.

September 19, 2022, 10:47 PM

Something strange happened in the office today.

During this afternoon's meeting, Xiao Wang from Quality Control handed in a report with problematic data. Usually, I would have said bluntly, "This is unacceptable. Redo it." But in that moment, I paused. I looked at his nervous expression and a thought suddenly hit me—is he afraid of something, too?

Instead, I said, "Let’s take a look together and see where things went wrong."

There was a moment of silence in the conference room. I could feel everyone looking at me. Xiao Wang’s shoulders relaxed a little.

After the meeting, I wrote in my notebook: "Maybe I should change my approach."

But now, sitting here in my workshop looking at these tools, I’m not sure if this is progress or a retreat.

The doctor’s words are still ringing in my ears: "Long-term medication, regular check-ups." I always thought that as long as I was strict and cautious enough, I could control everything. My work, my health—everything. But clearly, I was wrong. My body doesn't follow my orders. I can’t protect myself through perfect management.

Maybe what my subordinates see isn't my high standards, but my fear.

My wife said, "I knew you’d get this sooner or later." What did she mean by that? Did she see it coming all along? What about everyone else? I thought I had hidden it so well.

I don’t know how to tell the kids. Chen Hao is under so much pressure with his mortgage, and Chen Yu is busy with work in Guangzhou. Telling them would only worry them. But if I say nothing, isn't that a form of deception?

My hands are still shaking. I wiped down my tools, then wiped them again.

Maybe I’ve been using control to mask my vulnerability. Now that the vulnerability is exposed, I don’t know what’s left.

I have to go to the hospital tomorrow to pick up my prescription.

June 24, 2023, 9:45 PM

I was flipping through last year’s work log when I came across that note: "Retire within two years." The handwriting is clear, written in black felt-tip pen. The date was early 2022.

Now, it’s done.

I should feel satisfied. I acted according to plan, without a single deviation. That has always been my way. But sitting here in my study, looking at those words, I feel nothing.

No sense of fulfillment. No relief. Just a strange hollowness.

My wife asked me today if I wanted to go traveling with her, saying we should do it while we’re still in good health. I didn't give her a straight answer; I just said, "We’ll see." She looked a bit disappointed, but she didn't push it. This is the norm for us—she proposes, I delay, and in the end, nothing happens. Looking back now, I regret that attitude. But at the time, I truly couldn't think of what to say.

The point is—I don’t know what I want.

It’s strange. I’ve never been this kind of person. My plans were always clear: what to do and when, and what benchmarks needed to be met. But now that the plan is complete, I’ve fallen into a void I never anticipated.

Without work, who am I?

The question keeps looping in my head. Like a program I can't shut down.

I sat in the dark for a long time. My wife is already asleep. It’s quiet downstairs. I tried to clear my head as I usually do, but this time, logic seems to have failed me. I can list my identities—husband, father, retiree. But those words all feel hollow.

The image of those young engineers tearing up at the sharing session is still in my mind. They said my experience changed their perspective. At the time, I felt a sense of gratification. But now I wonder—what was that? Was it my value? Or just the footprint left by a passerby?

I’m not afraid of death. What I fear is that, until the moment I die, I will keep asking myself this question.

Tomorrow I still have to organize those tools to donate to the school. Perhaps that’s all I can do for now. Pass things on. Pass knowledge on. But I still don’t know if that’s enough.

Or rather, if it’s enough to make me someone.

If the opportunity arises, I might consider doing a technical sharing session at a vocational school.

Will continue pushing the proposal forward tomorrow.

October 12, 2022, 7:30 PM

Had a phone call with Chen Hao.

In the past, I’d always ask him if the mortgage was a burden, how the orders were coming along, or if his job was stable. I don’t know what came over me today, but the first thing out of my mouth was, "How have you been lately? Is there anything I can help with?" He seemed taken aback; there was a two-second silence before he answered.

He said work is exhausting, that sales is high-pressure, and sometimes he wonders if he’s doing the right thing. He’s lost. He was very candid, and I could hear the fatigue in his voice.

Before, I would have jumped straight in with advice, telling him exactly what he should do. But this time, I just said, "I understand. I’ve been through a stage like that, too."

Even I was a bit surprised by my own words. In forty years, I’ve never spoken to him like that.

He asked me when that was. I told him that when I was his age, I wasn't sure if I was making the right choices either. Back then, I was a quality inspector at the grassroots level, doing the same repetitive work every day, unable to see a way out. What I didn’t tell him was that I just kept going down that same path ever since, never changing course again.

He didn’t respond immediately, but I could sense something had shifted. It wasn't that superior-subordinate dynamic anymore; it felt more like... two people who had walked the same road.

After hanging up, I sat at my desk and looked at that medical report. The doctor said I’ll need long-term medication and a lifestyle change. I’ve been thinking about how to reverse this outcome through stricter management, just like I do at work—precise control, eliminating variables.

But while talking to Chen Hao, it suddenly occurred to me that maybe some things can’t be controlled. Some paths, once walked, are simply walked.

I need to rethink how I interact with them. Not supervising, not guiding. Maybe just... being there?

The thought makes me a little uneasy. I don't know if I still know how to do it.

More tomorrow.

Tuesday, September 13, 2022. Sunny.

I didn’t sleep well last night. I couldn't stop thinking about those numbers.

110 mg/dL. Stage 2 hypertension. Pre-metabolic syndrome. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis.

The doctor spoke calmly, as if reading a quality inspection report. I heard him clearly; I took in every word. And yet, it felt like I didn't truly hear it at all. I keep wondering how this is possible. I wake up at 6:30 every morning and go to bed at 11:00 PM. My diet is logged in my phone. I get regular check-ups every month. I have spent forty years maintaining order, maintaining control over everything.

My wife said, "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." I didn't answer her.

I spent the morning in the workshop, wiping down my tools one by one. The vernier caliper I bought in 1987, the set of Swiss precision screwdrivers, the vise... I wiped every single one clean. My movements were slow. I found myself wondering what would happen to these things if I collapsed.

My eldest son is in Shenzhen, struggling with his mortgage. My daughter is in Guangzhou, and her career isn't stable yet. I haven't told them about the diagnosis. What is there to say? That I need long-term medication? That my body has started to betray me?

I wrote a few things in my notebook. "What these tools have taught me." "Precision work has taught me more than just technique." I didn't keep writing.

I’m wondering if I should do a knowledge-sharing session before I retire. To systematically pass on everything from these forty years to the young people in the department. But I’m not sure how much time I have left. The thought makes me feel a sense of urgency, yet also a sense of powerlessness. I wrote "Sharing Session" on a sticky note, but the date box is still empty. I don’t know when would be appropriate. I don’t even know how much time I have left to be "appropriate."

The doctor prescribed some medicine. I have to take two kinds every day now. As I swallow them, I wonder if this is it—the beginning. The beginning of the decline.

I have always believed that precise management could prevent anything. But the medical report tells me that I cannot control everything.

I cannot control it.

How much longer?

Is it really too late?

July 10, 2023, 8:30 PM

Hao called while I was organizing my precision tools. My hands were still stained with engine oil, but I picked up.

He said there’s a new opportunity in Shenzhen—a sales director position for a major project. It sounds promising, but his voice was thick with hesitation. I could hear it.

In the past, I would have told him exactly what to do. I’d list the pros and cons and hand down a judgment. But today, I didn’t. I asked him: "Are you excited about this opportunity, or are you scared?"

There was a long silence. Then he said, "Dad, I’m both. I’m scared I won’t do a good job, and I’m scared of the mortgage pressure getting worse."

Listening to him, it suddenly hit me—I’ve always been supervising him, not accompanying him. Just like how I treat my subordinates at the department. I thought that was being responsible, but it was really just control.

I told him: "Then give it a try. I’ll be right behind you."

I surprised even myself when I said it. It’s not my style. But it was true. I really want to stand behind him, rather than standing in front of him telling him which way to go.

His tone changed. He sounded a bit more relaxed. We talked for a while longer; he shared some project details. I didn’t rush to judge; I just listened, occasionally asking a question.

Before hanging up, I told him: "Hao, I’m proud of you."

The moment the words left my mouth, I felt a pang of regret—I’ve said things like that far too rarely. But he said, "Thanks, Dad," and there was warmth in his voice.

After putting the phone down, I sat there for a long time. I was still holding that pair of pliers my father left me.

I think maybe retirement isn’t about losing anything, but finally having the time to do the things that truly matter. Not the achievements that can be quantified, but—being there. Listening. Saying the things I’ve kept bottled up inside.

My tools, my process improvements, my standards for perfect quality... those are all important. But Hao doesn’t need a perfect father; he needs a real one. A father willing to admit he isn’t omnipotent.

I’ll keep organizing the tools tomorrow. This time, it’s not just for the donation, but to remember—some things are more important than precision.

**Memo:**
Forty years. From entering the factory in 1982 to today. That corner desk in the office is finally empty.

While I was packing up this afternoon, the young engineers gave me a photo album filled with the projects we worked on together over the years. Flipping through it, I realized I’d forgotten some of the details, but they remembered everything.

June 26, 2023, 7:00 PM

I just got back from the chess and card room. My wife had already put dinner on the table. I didn't have much of an appetite and just had a few bites.

This is my third time at the club this week. What used to be just a weekend habit has now bled into Wednesdays and Fridays. One of my chess partners asked me, "How come you're here so often lately?" I didn't give him a straight answer; I just said, "Nothing better to do." But deep down, I know exactly what I’m doing—I’m trying to fill the gaps in my schedule.

It’s been nearly a month since I left my job. I still wake up on time every morning, but once I’m awake, I have no idea what to do with myself. It used to be work, but now? Playing chess, looking over financial statements, tidying up the tool shed... even all of that combined doesn't fill a whole day.

Another regular was talking today about how he went traveling in Yunnan after retiring and even took up photography. Someone else mentioned he’s teaching his grandson how to code. They all have things to do. I asked them, "What do you all do with yourselves now that you're retired?" The question came out awkward and stiff; I could feel it. Their advice was equally vague—"Just relax," "Find a hobby."

It’s not that I don’t understand the logic. The problem is, I’ve never had the habit of "relaxing." For forty years, every single day had a clear objective. Now that the goals are gone, I’m at a loss.

The days feel a bit empty.

That is my most honest feeling lately. It’s not melancholy, and it’s not depression; it’s just emptiness. It’s like a desk that’s been suddenly cleared off—all the files have been categorized and moved to the archives, leaving nothing but a bare, polished surface.

My wife asked me what TV show I wanted to watch tonight. I said, "Whatever." She sighed—a sigh I know well. She’s grown used to this version of me.

Maybe I need to do what she says and make a plan for myself. But what should that plan be? I’m starting to think it’s not a question of "what to do," but a question of "who I am." Without the title of manager, without the quality inspection reports, without the process optimizations... what is left of me?

Tomorrow, I’ll keep organizing the tool shed. At least the tools won't let me down.

Now, I’d much rather spend my time figuring out how to make low-sodium dishes more to her taste than staring at the kitchen scale and nitpicking over every gram.

To my shame, I used to think that as long as I had my calipers and data sheets in hand, I was in control of everything—I even tried to "optimize" Meifang’s health as if it were a production process. It wasn't until I received my own diagnosis of hypertension and sclerosis that I realized my cold supervision was actually torturing her with my own fears. That day, Meifang cried and told me that we don't live just to be afraid every day. It hit me then that the "wall of data" I had built over the last thirty-five years had actually cut off the very thing I should have cherished most: genuine care. Now I feel that instead of obsessing over a single spoonful of salt, I’d rather just go for a walk downstairs with her. After all, some things can’t be managed into place; they have to be nurtured through companionship.

Actually, it was that reunion with my old colleagues that really hit home for me. During dinner, Lao Zhang talked about his wife’s sudden stroke. He said he regretted spending all those years obsessed with production quotas in the workshop and acting like a bossy leader even after he got home. Listening to him, my heart started racing. I realized that although Meifang hasn't said much over the years, the warmth in her eyes has definitely faded. When I got home and saw her silhouette as she sat under the lamp mending old clothes, it suddenly hit me that those cold performance indicators are nowhere near as important as the simple, everyday life right in front of me. I realized that if I kept "managing" things this way, by the time I retired, I’d be left with nothing but myself and those useless ledgers. So, I decided to try and drop the act and start listening to what she has to say.

It all started with that trip to Shenzhen to visit my son. I had intended to pass down my management expertise to the younger generation, but Haozi told me straight up: "A home is a place for love, not a factory for logic." I kept chewing on those words all the way back. It hit me how Meifang has spent over thirty years walking on eggshells just to accommodate my temper—she couldn't even add a pinch of salt to a dish without checking my expression first. It left a bitter taste in my mouth. I realized that while I might be a competent master craftsman at the factory, I’ve been a failing husband at home. So, I decided to tuck all those spreadsheets away. I’m learning to be just an ordinary old man now—getting up early to buy her breakfast, taking her to the park after dinner to listen to some opera, and finally giving her the patience she’s been owed all these years.

As long as I see the curves leveling off, I feel that our lives are on a steady track, and my anxiety fades away.

This is mainly due to the influence of an old classmate of mine. After he retired, he took a specialized course in nutrition and suggested that I shouldn't just focus on medical indicators, but rather establish a "home health early-warning system." My daily habit of meticulously recording the oil and salt ratios in every meal is actually intended as a reference for my children. Even though they aren't with me right now, when they eventually reach my age and look back through these detailed food logs, they’ll understand the importance of a disciplined lifestyle. For me, it’s also a way to keep them in my heart.

I’m ashamed to admit that I’ve grown used to using these charts and percentages to give myself a sense of "courage." Seeing a precise figure like a 3.7% rise in blood pressure is unsettling, yet as long as I can quantify it, I feel like things are still under my control. In truth, I know that even if I measured Meifang’s daily salt intake down to the gram, I can’t stop time from moving on. This obsession is likely just a way to mask my inner fear of losing control.

To be honest, I’ve spent my entire life dealing with standards, and what I dread most is the "good enough" mentality. It wasn't until I attended that expert lecture in the community that I realized how crucial meticulous management is for us as we age. Now, I even go as far as recording my deep sleep duration and heart rate fluctuations on my desk calendar. This precision in capturing subtle bodily changes allows me to anticipate potential risks ahead of time. For me, these flickering numbers aren't a burden; they are the coordinates that guide my life back onto the right track.

That’s why I hide away in the workshop, wiping down my measuring tools over and over, or balancing the household accounts until every cent is accounted for. Only when I’m looking at things I can precisely control does that sense of powerlessness from growing old finally start to settle down a bit.

How is it that now, with the confirmed diagnosis and prescription in hand, you’ve become so quiet and started Cooperating with the long-term medication?

I used to think that as long as I was rigorous enough and kept a tight enough grip on the data, I could somehow keep the inevitable realities of life and aging at bay. Back then, I was desperately digging through research, but honestly, it was just because I refused to accept it. But when I actually sat in the car, staring at that prescription marked "long-term medication" and re-reading it three times, it finally hit me: the human body is a precision machine, but it’s not something I can fully control just by writing a few process improvements. That sense of powerlessness is a real blow, but since it’s now a settled fact, there’s no point in dwelling on it. Following the regimen and taking my pills—maybe that’s the only "precision management" I have left.

qa_items.question: Manager Chen, looking back over the past year or so, you used to rely so heavily on precise data and strict processes to manage your life. Now, what do you think the sense of "being in control" truly means to a person?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put it? I used to think that if I could keep my blood pressure fluctuations within 3.7% or perfectly align my heart rate on the treadmill, I’d have a firm grip on life. But ever since that pre-metabolic syndrome diagnosis—when my hands were shaking as I reached for the prescription—I’ve realized that some things just can’t be calculated. Now, I feel that true control isn't about obsessing over irreversible signs of aging. It’s about being able to settle your mind and cook a simple, healthy meal for your family even when you realize how fragile you are, or donating a lifetime’s collection of precision tools to a technical school—leaving something useful behind in a world that’s spinning out of control.

evidence.statement: Upon receiving his medical checkup results, Chen Guilin immediately created a comparison table of his physical data over the past five years and repeatedly calculated the percentage increase in his blood pressure (3.7%). This demonstrates that he has begun using data to quantify his anxiety over aging.

Chen Guilin stopped exercising on the treadmill due to heart rate fluctuations and manually verified the machine's data, demonstrating that his preoccupation with bodily metrics has reached an obsessive level—he simply cannot tolerate any uncertainty or data deviation.

When Chen Guilin heard terms like "long-term medication" and "pre-metabolic syndrome," his rational self tried to remain calm, but the trembling of his hands and the repeated stabbing at the prescription slip betrayed a deep-seated fear of losing control over his own body.

As he wiped his tools, Chen Guilin found himself trapped in a recursive loop of thought: he had always believed that precise lifestyle management was the key to maintaining health, yet the doctor’s words—that this was not enough—made him begin to doubt whether the past forty years of discipline had all been in vain.

I used to try to quantify every cent spent on healthcare and every minute of exercise, believing that as long as the data looked good, my life was on track. But after my spouse’s accidental fall, I realized that true control comes from learning to leave "white space." Now, I make time for a community gardening group, tending to plants that don’t follow orders and grow as they please. Watching them change naturally with the seasons, I no longer obsess over minor fluctuations in my health metrics. Instead, I’ve learned to maintain inner calm and order by simply going with the flow of nature.

To me, a sense of control means having foresight into future risks. After that hospitalization for acute gastritis, I began systematically organizing all our family’s insurance policies and asset lists, sorting the once-scattered documents into labeled folders. These days, I no longer obsess over hitting daily step goals or tracking my heart rate with precision. Instead, I hold regular video calls with my children, who work in other cities, to clearly walk them through any changes at home and our plans for the future. This honest communication gives me the feeling that even if life fluctuates, the foundation remains solid.

In fact, I only gained a true sense of control the moment I realized my physical stamina could no longer keep up with the younger generation and took the initiative to apply for a transfer to the internal training department. I used to stare at those quality control charts, feeling that even a one-percent drop was a personal failure. But later, I discovered that teaching new apprentices how to dismantle complex mechanical structures and watching them complete assemblies independently under my guidance felt far more grounding than cold pass-rate figures. It allowed me to rediscover my place within the collective.

qa_items.question: After all these ups and downs, how do you feel you’ve changed from that "neat-to-a-fault" manager sitting in the corner?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? I used to believe that as long as my plans were detailed enough and my standards strict enough, life would be under control. But now I realize those cold data points and spreadsheets were actually just walls I built to hide my fear. When Meifang looked at me with tearful eyes and said, "Living isn't about being afraid," I realized just how wrong I had been. Nowadays, while I still habitually keep my tools spotless, I’ve learned to let go of that pathological need to supervise. I’m learning to admit my own vulnerability and how to truly be there for my family, instead of just making demands of them.

qa_items.question: All in all, if a junior colleague—someone just like you, nearing retirement and a relentless perfectionist—were to come to you for advice, how would you suggest they balance the relationship between "professional achievement" and "personal vulnerability"?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put it? This has actually been the hardest lesson of my life. I used to think that as long as I set the processes in stone and kept the standards airtight, everything would be under control. But later I realized that people aren't machines; even the most precise instruments have moments of failure. I would tell him: stop trying to mask your inner anxiety by controlling everything. There is no shame in admitting that you will grow old, get sick, and feel powerless. Compared to those cold quality metrics, spending more time listening to your family’s chatter and learning to loosen the "vise" you’ve been gripping so tightly—that is where you truly belong.

evidence.statement: Chen Guilin publicly and harshly reprimanded a subordinate over a mere formatting error, using extreme rhetoric like "details determine product quality, and product quality determines the company's survival" to make his point—a clear sign that his need for control over his work has escalated to a whole new level.

When his wife said, "We are living together, not so we can live in fear every day," Chen Guilin realized that his control and surveillance were hurting her rather than helping—marking his first true moment of reflection on his controlling behavior.

Before leaving the consultation room, Chen Guilin turned back and asked, "Is this diagnosis certain?"—it was his way of trying to regain control by confirming the facts, but the doctor's affirmative reply caused his shoulders to visibly slump as he realized this was a reality he could not change.

As he wiped his tools, Chen Guilin found himself trapped in a recursive loop of thought: he had always believed that health could be maintained through meticulous life management, yet the doctor’s words suggested otherwise—leaving him to wonder if the past forty years of discipline had all been in vain.

It ended with me collapsing during a morning run; waking up and staring at the ceiling, I finally realized that physical health is the "1" that makes everything else possible. I’d tell him not to obsess over those few percentage points of growth. Instead, carve out an hour every day to take a walk in the park or pick up that instrument he abandoned in his youth. When you can find fulfillment in a melody or a sunset, you’ll stop letting the minor gains and losses at work fuel your self-doubt.
